title: 10  Random Slopes
section: 11.5 Model Comparison: Do You Need Random Slopes?
text:
11.5 Model Comparison: Do You Need Random Slopes?
Adding random slopes adds parameters and complexity. You should include them when:

Theory predicts heterogeneous effects: Do you have a reason to believe the relationship varies across units?
The LRT supports them: The model with random slopes fits significantly better than the model without.
The design requires them: In within-subject experimental designs, random slopes for experimental conditions are almost always needed (see Module 5).

title: 10  Random Slopes
text:
11.6 Recommended Reading







Topic
Citation




Random slopes in experimental designs
Barr, D. J., Levy, R., Scheepers, C., & Tily, H. J. (2013). Random effects structure for confirmatory hypothesis testing: Keep it maximal. Journal of Memory and Language, 68(3), 255–278.


When random slopes matter
Heisig, J. P., & Schaeffer, M. (2019). Why you should always include a random slope for the lower-level variable involved in a cross-level interaction. European Sociological Review, 35(2), 258–279.

title: 11  Fixed vs. Random: When to Use Which
text: 12 Fixed vs. Random Effects: When to Use Which
crumbs: 11  Fixed vs. Random: When to Use Which

title: 11  Fixed vs. Random: When to Use Which
text:
12.1 The Most Frequently Asked Question
If you have been following along, you have noticed that fixed effects and random effects make different assumptions, answer slightly different questions, and have different costs and benefits. The obvious question is: which one should I use?
The honest answer is: it depends on your data and your scientific question. But it is not arbitrary. There are principled criteria for making this decision.
crumbs: 11  Fixed vs. Random: When to Use Which

title: 11  Fixed vs. Random: When to Use Which
text:
12.2 A Side-by-Side Comparison








Property
Fixed Effects
Random Intercepts




Assumption about group effects
Arbitrary constants to be estimated
Drawn from a normal distribution


Correlation with predictors
Allowed (and corrected for)
Must be approximately zero


Between-unit variation
Removed entirely
Modeled and retained


Unit-level predictors
Cannot estimate
Can estimate


Predictions for new units
Not possible
Possible (using variance components)


Efficiency
Less efficient (uses more df)
More efficient if RE assumption holds


Robustness
Robust to correlated random effects
Biased if RE-predictor correlation exists
crumbs: 11  Fixed vs. Random: When to Use Which

title: 11  Fixed vs. Random: When to Use Which
text:
12.3 The Decisive Criterion
The most important question is whether the random effects (unit-level deviations) are correlated with your predictors.

If yes: Random effects models are biased. Use fixed effects.
If no: Random effects models are more efficient. Use them.

The Hausman test formalizes this check (covered in Part 2). But you should also think about it substantively:
Example 1 — Use FE: You are studying whether neighborhood green space predicts depression. People choose where to live based on many factors — income, family ties, preferences — that also affect mental health. The random effects (neighborhood-level deviations in mental health) are surely correlated with neighborhood characteristics. Use fixed effects.
Example 2 — Use RE: You run a multi-site experiment where participants at 20 different universities complete a survey. The sites were chosen roughly at random, and the random effects (site-level deviations in mean response) are unlikely to correlate with your experimental manipulation. Use random effects.
crumbs: 11  Fixed vs. Random: When to Use Which

title: 11  Fixed vs. Random: When to Use Which
text:
12.4 A Third Option: Hybrid Models
In many cases, you actually want both: the within-unit consistency of fixed effects and the ability to model between-unit effects. This is achievable using cluster-mean centering, covered in Module 2.
The hybrid approach: 1. Center the predictor around each unit’s mean (within-unit component) 2. Include the unit mean as a separate predictor (between-unit component) 3. Use a random-intercept model
This separates within and between effects explicitly, avoids the confound problem, and retains the ability to study unit-level variation. It is often the best default for panel and longitudinal data.
crumbs: 11  Fixed vs. Random: When to Use Which

title: 11  Fixed vs. Random: When to Use Which
section: 12.5 Scientific Questions Also Drive the Choice
text:
12.5 Scientific Questions Also Drive the Choice
Beyond the statistical criteria, your scientific question matters:







Question
Preferred Model




What is the causal effect of X on Y within units?
Fixed effects


How does X relate to Y on average, accounting for clustering?
Random effects


How much do units vary in their Y levels?
Random effects (ICC)


How much do units vary in the X-Y relationship?
Random slopes


Can I predict a new unit’s Y from its X?
Random effects


Does unit-level characteristic Z predict Y?
Random effects
crumbs: 11  Fixed vs. Random: When to Use Which

title: 11  Fixed vs. Random: When to Use Which
text:
12.6 What About Bayesian Approaches?
Bayesian mixed-effects models (brms package) extend the random-effects framework with explicit priors on variance components. They are particularly useful when:

The model is complex and frequentist estimation has convergence problems
You want proper uncertainty quantification for variance components (frequentist intervals for variances can be poorly behaved near zero)
You want to incorporate prior knowledge about reasonable effect sizes

The conceptual structure is identical to the frequentist random-effects model. The assumptions are the same. The key difference is in how uncertainty is expressed and how parameters are estimated.
crumbs: 11  Fixed vs. Random: When to Use Which

title: 11  Fixed vs. Random: When to Use Which
text:
12.7 Summary Decision Tree
Is the random effect correlated with your predictors?
├── Yes → Fixed Effects
└── No or Unknown →
    ├── Do you want to estimate unit-level predictor effects?
    │   ├── Yes → Random Effects
    └── ├── Do you care about between-unit variance (ICC)?
        │   ├── Yes → Random Effects
        └── └── Do you want predictions for new units?
                ├── Yes → Random Effects
                └── No → Either (use Hausman test to choose)
crumbs: 11  Fixed vs. Random: When to Use Which

title: 12  Three-Level Structures
text: 13 Three-Level Structures

title: 12  Three-Level Structures
text:
13.1 When Two Levels Aren’t Enough
Real organizational and social data is almost always more than two levels deep. Patients are nested within therapists, who are nested within clinics. Students are nested within classrooms, which are nested within schools, which are nested within districts. Employees are nested within teams, which are nested within departments, which are nested within firms.
A three-level model extends the random-effects framework by adding a third random effect — one for each of the three levels of the hierarchy.

title: 12  Three-Level Structures
text:
13.2 The Model
Level 1 (observations within units within groups): \[Y_{ijk} = \beta_{0jk} + \beta_1 X_{ijk} + e_{ijk}\]
Level 2 (units within groups): \[\beta_{0jk} = \gamma_{00k} + u_{0jk}\]
Level 3 (groups): \[\gamma_{00k} = \delta_{000} + v_{0k}\]
Combined: \[Y_{ijk} = \delta_{000} + \beta_1 X_{ijk} + v_{0k} + u_{0jk} + e_{ijk}\]
Three variance components: \(\sigma^2_v\) (level-3 variance), \(\sigma^2_u\) (level-2 variance), \(\sigma^2_e\) (level-1 variance).


Code
# Simulate: patients within therapists within clinics
n_clinics    <- 8
n_therapists <- 4  # per clinic
n_patients   <- 10 # per therapist

clinic_effects    <- rnorm(n_clinics, 0, 4)     # clinic-level variance
therapist_effects <- rnorm(n_clinics * n_therapists, 0, 3)  # therapist-level

df_3l <- expand_grid(
  clinic    = 1:n_clinics,
  therapist = 1:n_therapists,
  patient   = 1:n_patients
) |>
  mutate(
    therapist_id    = (clinic - 1) * n_therapists + therapist,
    sessions        = round(runif(n(), 5, 20)),
    outcome         = 50 + 0.5 * sessions +
                      clinic_effects[clinic] +
                      therapist_effects[therapist_id] +
                      rnorm(n(), 0, 5),
    clinic          = factor(clinic, labels = paste0("C", 1:n_clinics)),
    therapist_f     = factor(therapist_id)
  )

# Three-level model: patients in therapists in clinics
model_3l <- lmer(outcome ~ sessions + (1 | clinic/therapist_f), data = df_3l)
summary(model_3l)

title: 12  Three-Level Structures
text:
13.3 ICC for Three-Level Data
In a three-level model, there are two ICCs:
Level-3 ICC: Proportion of variance attributable to the highest-level unit (e.g., clinic): \[\text{ICC}_3 = \frac{\sigma^2_v}{\sigma^2_v + \sigma^2_u + \sigma^2_e}\]
Level-2 ICC: Proportion of variance attributable to level-2 units and above (e.g., therapist or above): \[\text{ICC}_2 = \frac{\sigma^2_v + \sigma^2_u}{\sigma^2_v + \sigma^2_u + \sigma^2_e}\]

title: 12  Three-Level Structures
text:
13.4 The Assumptions of Three-Level Models
All assumptions from the two-level model carry over. Additionally:






WarningAssumption: Correct Level Specification



What it means: The model must correctly identify which units belong to which level. If therapists are incorrectly attributed to clinics (data entry errors, therapists who work at multiple clinics), the variance components will be misattributed.
How to check: Cross-tabulate your level-2 and level-3 identifiers. Each level-2 unit should belong to exactly one level-3 unit (in a pure hierarchy).


Code
df_3l |>
  distinct(clinic, therapist_id) |>
  group_by(therapist_id) |>
  filter(n() > 1)  # should return 0 rows for a pure hierarchy






clinic
therapist_id
















WarningAssumption: Sample Size at Each Level



What it means: Variance components at higher levels require sufficient numbers of level-3 units to be estimated reliably. As a rough guide, you want at least 20–30 units at each level where you are estimating a random effect.
Why it matters: With only 5 clinics, the clinic-level variance estimate is very noisy. Confidence intervals for level-3 variance components are wide with small level-3 samples.
How to check: Count units at each level. Be cautious interpreting level-3 variance with fewer than 20 level-3 units.

title: 12  Three-Level Structures
text:
13.5 Adding Level-2 and Level-3 Predictors
One of the major advantages of the three-level model is the ability to include predictors at each level and examine cross-level interactions.


Code
# Add clinic-level predictor (e.g., clinic size)
df_3l <- df_3l |>
  group_by(clinic) |>
  mutate(clinic_size = n_distinct(therapist_f) * n_patients + rnorm(1, 0, 2)) |>
  ungroup() |>
  group_by(clinic, therapist_f) |>
  mutate(therapist_experience = round(runif(1, 1, 20))) |>
  ungroup()

model_3l_pred <- lmer(
  outcome ~ sessions + therapist_experience + clinic_size +
    (1 | clinic/therapist_f),
  data = df_3l
)

broom.mixed::tidy(model_3l_pred, effects = "fixed") |>

title: 13  Crossed Random Effects
text: 14 Crossed Random Effects

title: 13  Crossed Random Effects
text:
14.1 The Problem with Pure Hierarchies
Pure hierarchies assume each lower-level unit belongs to exactly one higher-level unit. But in many real data structures — especially experimental designs in psychology — units belong to multiple grouping factors simultaneously.
The classic example: A reaction time experiment. 40 participants each respond to 80 words. Each observation belongs to one participant AND one word. Participants are not nested within words. Words are not nested within participants. Both grouping factors are fully crossed.
If you only include a random effect for participants (as most psychologists do), you are assuming that all variance due to words is captured by the residual. But words differ systematically — some words are rare and hard to process; others are frequent and easy. This word-level variance inflates your residuals and biases your standard errors.
Judd, Westfall, and Kenny (2012, 2017) showed that failing to include random effects for stimuli is one of the most widespread sources of Type I error inflation in social and cognitive psychology.

title: 13  Crossed Random Effects
text:
14.2 The Model
For crossed random effects with participants (\(p\)) and stimuli (\(s\)):
\[Y_{ps} = \gamma_{00} + \beta_1 X_{ps} + u_{0p} + w_{0s} + \varepsilon_{ps}\]
Where: - \(u_{0p} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2_u)\): participant random intercepts - \(w_{0s} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2_w)\): stimulus random intercepts - \(\varepsilon_{ps}\): residual


Code
# Simulate a lexical decision task
n_participants <- 40
n_words        <- 80

participant_effects <- rnorm(n_participants, 0, 50)   # people differ in baseline RT
word_effects        <- rnorm(n_words, 0, 40)           # words differ in baseline RT
word_frequency      <- runif(n_words, 1, 10)           # word frequency predictor

df_ld <- expand_grid(
  participant = 1:n_participants,
  word        = 1:n_words
) |>
  mutate(
    participant_eff = participant_effects[participant],
    word_eff        = word_effects[word],
    frequency       = word_frequency[word],
    RT              = 550 - 15 * frequency +
                      participant_eff + word_eff +
                      rnorm(n(), 0, 30),
    participant     = factor(participant),
    word            = factor(word)
  )

# Crossed random effects model
model_crossed <- lmer(RT ~ frequency + (1 | participant) + (1 | word),
                      data = df_ld)

summary(model_crossed)

section: 14.3 Comparing: With and Without Word Random Effects
text:
14.3 Comparing: With and Without Word Random Effects


Code
# Without word random effects (wrong)
model_no_words <- lmer(RT ~ frequency + (1 | participant), data = df_ld)

# With word random effects (correct)
model_with_words <- lmer(RT ~ frequency + (1 | participant) + (1 | word), data = df_ld)

# Compare standard errors for the frequency effect
wrong_se  <- broom.mixed::tidy(model_no_words)[2, "std.error"][[1]]
correct_se <- broom.mixed::tidy(model_with_words)[2, "std.error"][[1]]

tibble(
  Model = c("Without word RE", "With word RE"),
  Frequency_SE = c(wrong_se, correct_se)
) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "Standard error of frequency effect: ignoring vs. modeling word-level variance")



Standard error of frequency effect: ignoring vs. modeling word-level variance


Model
Frequency_SE




Without word RE

With word RE
1.740





When word-level variance is ignored, the standard error for frequency is smaller — the model is treating word-level variance as residual noise, which artificially deflates uncertainty. This produces anti-conservative inference: p-values are too small.

title: 13  Crossed Random Effects
section: 14.4 The Assumptions of Crossed Random-Effects Models
text:
14.4 The Assumptions of Crossed Random-Effects Models






WarningAssumption 1: Independence of Participant and Stimulus Random Effects



What it means: The random effect for participant \(p\) and the random effect for word \(w\) are assumed to be independent of each other. Person 7 having an unusually fast RT is not related to whether word 42 is unusually easy.
How to check: In a balanced fully-crossed design, this is usually reasonable. In unbalanced designs (not all participants see all stimuli), check whether missingness is systematic.








WarningAssumption 2: Random Effects are Independent of the Predictor



What it means: As with all random-effects models, the random intercepts for participants and words should be uncorrelated with the predictors. Word frequency should not predict which words were seen by which participants (in a balanced design, it cannot).
Why this is usually satisfied in experiments: In a fully-crossed, counterbalanced design, every participant sees every word — so assignment is orthogonal to word characteristics. This is one reason well-designed experiments support random-effects modeling.








WarningAssumption 3: The Same Model Applies to All Participant-Stimulus Pairs



What it means: The frequency effect is assumed to be the same for all participants (unless a random slope for frequency by participants is included). If some participants are more sensitive to frequency than others, a random slope for frequency by participants is needed.
How to check: As with all random-slope decisions, use likelihood ratio tests and theoretical reasoning.


Code
# Can add random slopes for frequency by participants
model_maximal <- lmer(RT ~ frequency + (frequency | participant) + (1 | word),
                      data   = df_ld,
                      control = lmerControl(optimizer = "bobyqa"))

anova(model_with_words, model_maximal)

title: 13  Crossed Random Effects
text:
14.5 The “Keep It Maximal” Debate
Barr et al. (2013) argued that you should always include the maximal random effects structure — every random slope that is theoretically justified by the design. This minimizes Type I error.
Bates et al. (2015) and Matuschek et al. (2017) pushed back, arguing that maximal models frequently fail to converge, and that parsimonious models (with only the random effects supported by the data) often provide better estimates.
The current consensus: include random slopes for all within-unit manipulations (this is critical), but simplify the correlation structure or remove slopes not supported by the data if the model fails to converge.
This debate is covered in depth in Module 5.

text:
14.6 Recommended Reading







Topic
Citation




Subjects and items as crossed random effects

title: 14  Overview
text: Module 2: Centering & Between/Within Effects

title: 14  Overview
text:
14.1 The Subtlest Assumption
You have built a mixed-effects model, checked your residuals, verified convergence, and are ready to interpret your coefficients. There is one more question you need to answer before you do:
What does your predictor’s value actually mean?
This sounds almost philosophical, but it has concrete, testable consequences. A predictor like “daily sleep hours” can mean two different things in a within-person longitudinal dataset:

Between-person effect: People who tend to sleep more also tend to have higher mood (perhaps because of a third factor — stable health, lifestyle, genetics — or because sleep causally improves mood on average across people)
Within-person effect: On days when this specific person sleeps more than their own average, their mood is higher

These two effects are not the same. They can point in opposite directions. They answer different scientific questions. And the standard random-intercept model, without careful centering, conflates them.
Module 2 is about learning to see the difference — and to model it explicitly.

title: 14  Overview
text:
14.2 What This Module Covers
Part 1: Centering Your Variables introduces grand-mean centering (GMC) and cluster-mean centering (CMC), explains what each does to the interpretation of your coefficients, and provides a decision framework.
Part 2: Between-Unit vs. Within-Unit Effects shows how to simultaneously estimate both effects in a single model — the “Mundlak device” — and when the between and within effects diverge.
Part 3: Simpson’s Paradox uses vivid examples to show how the relationship at the aggregate level can be completely opposite to the relationship at the individual level. This is not a statistical curiosity — it is a systematic feature of nested data that researchers frequently misinterpret.

title: 14  Overview
text:
14.3 The Core Insight
Standard regression assumes your predictor has one meaning. In nested data, it has (at least) two: a within-unit meaning and a between-unit meaning. Centering is the tool that separates them.

“What do your means mean?” — this question, asked before every analysis, will save you from some of the most common and consequential errors in social science research.

title: 15  Centering Your Variables
text: 16 Centering Your Variables

title: 15  Centering Your Variables
text:
16.1 Why Centering Matters
When you include a predictor in a mixed-effects model, what exactly is it measuring? The answer depends entirely on how you center it. This is not a cosmetic choice — it changes the meaning of your coefficients.

title: 15  Centering Your Variables
text:
16.2 Grand-Mean Centering (GMC)
Grand-mean centering subtracts the overall mean from each observation: \[X^{GMC}_{ij} = X_{ij} - \bar{X}_{..}\]
In a GMC model, the slope reflects a mix of within-unit and between-unit variation. The intercept now reflects the expected outcome when \(X\) equals its grand mean — a meaningful value for continuous predictors.
GMC is appropriate when: - You have a single-level dataset (no nesting) - You are not trying to separate within- and between-person effects - You want the intercept to be meaningful (grand mean of X rather than X = 0)

title: 15  Centering Your Variables
text:
16.3 Cluster-Mean Centering (CMC)
Cluster-mean centering (also called person-mean centering or within-person centering) subtracts each unit’s mean from their own observations: \[X^{CMC}_{ij} = X_{ij} - \bar{X}_{j.}\]
The centered variable now captures only within-unit deviations. For a daily diary study, \(X^{CMC}_{ij}\) = “how much more/less did person \(j\) sleep on day \(i\) compared to their own average?”
The between-unit effect of X can be recovered separately by including the unit mean (\(\bar{X}_{j.}\)) as a level-2 predictor.


Code
# Simulate: people differ in average sleep AND have within-person variation
n_people <- 50
n_days   <- 7

# Between-person: high sleepers have high mood (confound: healthy lifestyle)
person_sleep_avg <- rnorm(n_people, 7, 1)
person_mood_base <- 40 + 5 * person_sleep_avg + rnorm(n_people, 0, 5)

# Within-person: on high-sleep days, mood is slightly higher
df <- expand_grid(person = 1:n_people, day = 1:n_days) |>
  mutate(
    sleep_raw   = person_sleep_avg[person] + rnorm(n(), 0, 1),
    mood        = person_mood_base[person] + 2 * (sleep_raw - person_sleep_avg[person]) +
                  rnorm(n(), 0, 4),
    person      = factor(person)
  )

# Compute centering variables
df <- df |>
  group_by(person) |>
  mutate(
    sleep_person_mean = mean(sleep_raw),        # L2 variable (between)
    sleep_cmc         = sleep_raw - sleep_person_mean  # within-person deviation
  ) |>
  ungroup() |>
  mutate(
    sleep_gmc = sleep_raw - mean(sleep_raw)     # grand-mean centered
  )

# Three models
m_uncentered <- lmer(mood ~ sleep_raw + (1 | person), data = df)
m_gmc        <- lmer(mood ~ sleep_gmc + (1 | person), data = df)
m_cmc        <- lmer(mood ~ sleep_cmc + sleep_person_mean + (1 | person), data = df)

# Compare the sleep coefficient across models
results <- bind_rows(
  broom.mixed::tidy(m_uncentered, effects = "fixed") |> filter(term == "sleep_raw") |> mutate(model = "Uncentered"),
  broom.mixed::tidy(m_gmc, effects = "fixed")        |> filter(term == "sleep_gmc") |> mutate(model = "GMC"),
  broom.mixed::tidy(m_cmc, effects = "fixed")        |> filter(term == "sleep_cmc") |> mutate(model = "CMC (within)"),
  broom.mixed::tidy(m_cmc, effects = "fixed")        |> filter(term == "sleep_person_mean") |> mutate(model = "CMC (between)")
) |>
  select(model, term, estimate, std.error, p.value)

knitr::kable(results, digits = 3,
  caption = "Centering changes what the sleep coefficient means")

Reading the table:

Uncentered / GMC: The slope is a blend of within- and between-person effects. Its magnitude is pulled by the larger between-person correlation.
CMC (within): Close to the true within-person effect (~2). This answers “on days when you sleep more than usual, is your mood better?”
CMC (between): The between-person effect (~5). This answers “do people who generally sleep more also have higher baseline mood?” — a question contaminated by lifestyle confounds.

The within-effect and the between-effect are different numbers with different interpretations. A model that conflates them gives you neither correctly.






TipWhen to Use Each




Grand-mean centering: When you only care about the overall relationship, not within/between decomposition, and you want a meaningful intercept.
Cluster-mean centering: Whenever you have a within-unit predictor and want to explicitly separate within-unit from between-unit effects. Default recommendation for longitudinal and panel data.
Uncentered: When X = 0 is a meaningful value AND you do not need to separate within/between effects (rare in nested data).

title: 15  Centering Your Variables
text:
16.4 Assumptions That Centering Affects
Centering is not just an interpretive choice — it affects which assumptions your model makes:






WarningAssumption: No Conflation of Within and Between Effects



If you include a predictor without CMC in a random-effects model, the model assumes the within-unit and between-unit relationships are equal. If they are not equal — as in the example above — this is a misspecification that biases both estimates.
How to test: Fit both the uncentered and the CMC model. If the within-person and between-person coefficients from the CMC model differ substantially, the conflation assumption is violated.

title: 16  Between-Unit vs. Within-Unit Effects
text: 17 Between-Unit vs. Within-Unit Effects

title: 16  Between-Unit vs. Within-Unit Effects
text:
17.1 The Same Variable, Two Stories
Consider studying whether a nation’s GDP predicts its citizens’ wellbeing. You have survey data from 60 countries over 10 years.
The between-country question: Do richer countries have happier citizens on average? The within-country question: In years when a country’s GDP grows, do its citizens report higher wellbeing?
These are completely different empirical questions. The between-country question picks up all stable differences between rich and poor countries (culture, institutions, climate, history). The within-country question is closer to a causal estimate of the effect of economic growth on wellbeing, net of these stable differences.
Research consistently finds that the answers are different: rich countries are happier than poor ones, but within a country, economic booms correlate with wellbeing only modestly (the “Easterlin Paradox”).

title: 16  Between-Unit vs. Within-Unit Effects
section: 17.2 Separating the Effects: The Mundlak Device
text:
17.2 Separating the Effects: The Mundlak Device
The cleanest way to separate within- and between-unit effects is to include both in the same model:

Cluster-mean center the predictor (captures within-unit variation)
Include the cluster mean as a separate level-2 predictor (captures between-unit variation)



Code
# Simulate country panel data
n_countries <- 40
n_years     <- 10

# Between: rich countries ARE happier (partly for other reasons)
country_gdp_avg  <- rnorm(n_countries, 50, 15)
country_wellbeing_base <- 50 + 0.6 * country_gdp_avg + rnorm(n_countries, 0, 8)

# Within: growth has only modest effect on wellbeing
df_gdp <- expand_grid(country = 1:n_countries, year = 1:n_years) |>
  mutate(
    gdp_raw       = country_gdp_avg[country] + rnorm(n(), 0, 5),
    wellbeing     = country_wellbeing_base[country] +
                    0.15 * (gdp_raw - country_gdp_avg[country]) +
                    rnorm(n(), 0, 4),
    country       = factor(country)
  ) |>
  group_by(country) |>
  mutate(
    gdp_mean = mean(gdp_raw),
    gdp_cmc  = gdp_raw - gdp_mean
  ) |>
  ungroup()

# Pooled (mixes effects)
m_pooled <- lmer(wellbeing ~ gdp_raw + (1 | country), data = df_gdp)

# Mundlak: both within and between
m_mundlak <- lmer(wellbeing ~ gdp_cmc + gdp_mean + (1 | country), data = df_gdp)

bind_rows(
  broom.mixed::tidy(m_pooled,  effects = "fixed") |> mutate(model = "Pooled"),
  broom.mixed::tidy(m_mundlak, effects = "fixed") |> mutate(model = "Mundlak")
) |>
  select(model, term, estimate, std.error, p.value) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3, caption = "Within vs. between effects of GDP on wellbeing")

The Mundlak model reveals: - Within effect (gdp_cmc ≈ 0.15): When a country’s GDP is above its own average, wellbeing is modestly higher. Small effect. - Between effect (gdp_mean ≈ 0.6): Countries with higher average GDP have substantially higher wellbeing — but this partly reflects other stable advantages of wealthy nations.
The pooled model blends these two stories into one coefficient that misrepresents both.

title: 16  Between-Unit vs. Within-Unit Effects
section: 17.3 The Assumption Test: Are Within and Between Effects Equal?
text:
17.3 The Assumption Test: Are Within and Between Effects Equal?
The formal test for whether within and between effects are equal is simple: if the CMC model’s within coefficient (\(\beta_{within}\)) and between coefficient (\(\beta_{between}\)) are statistically indistinguishable, you can use the simpler pooled model. If they differ, they must be separated.


Code
library(car)
# Test: within effect = between effect?
linearHypothesis(m_mundlak, "gdp_cmc = gdp_mean")

A significant test (p < 0.05) means within and between effects differ — the pooled model is misspecified.

title: 17  Simpson’s Paradox
text: 18 Simpson’s Paradox

title: 17  Simpson’s Paradox
text:
18.1 When the Aggregate Lies
Simpson’s Paradox occurs when a relationship that exists in the aggregate data reverses when you look within subgroups. It is not a statistical quirk — it is a direct consequence of ignoring nesting, and it is surprisingly common.


Code
# Classic demonstration: grades and study time, but students vary by program
# Hard program students study more AND have lower grades (because the program is harder)
n_students  <- 200
n_programs  <- 5

program_difficulty <- c(2, 4, 6, 8, 10)   # harder programs
program_study_avg  <- c(8, 7, 6, 5, 4)    # harder programs -> students study MORE

students <- tibble(
  student  = 1:n_students,
  program  = rep(1:n_programs, each = n_students / n_programs),
  study    = program_study_avg[program] + rnorm(n_students, 0, 0.8),
  grade    = 80 - 3 * program_difficulty[program] + 2 * (study - program_study_avg[program]) +
             rnorm(n_students, 0, 3),
  program_label = factor(program, labels = paste("Program", 1:n_programs))
)

# Overall relationship: NEGATIVE (studying more -> lower grades??)
m_pooled <- lm(grade ~ study, data = students)

# Within-program: POSITIVE
m_within <- lmer(grade ~ study + (1 | program_label), data = students)

p1 <- ggplot(students, aes(x = study, y = grade)) +
  geom_point(aes(color = program_label), alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", color = "black", linewidth = 1.5) +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set1") +
  labs(title = "Pooled: studying MORE predicts LOWER grades?!",
       x = "Study hours/week", y = "Grade", color = "Program") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

p2 <- ggplot(students, aes(x = study, y = grade, color = program_label)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, linewidth = 1) +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set1") +
  labs(title = "Within program: studying MORE predicts HIGHER grades",
       x = "Study hours/week", y = "Grade", color = "Program") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

library(patchwork)
p1 / p2









The pooled relationship is negative: students who study more get lower grades. That looks like studying causes harm!
The truth: students in harder programs study more AND get lower grades — because the programs are harder. Within any given program, studying more helps. The aggregate correlation is driven entirely by the confound between program difficulty and study time.
This is Simpson’s Paradox, and it happens in your data.

title: 17  Simpson’s Paradox
text:
18.2 Real-World Examples
Berkeley admissions (1973): Across departments, women were admitted at lower rates than men. Within departments, women were admitted at the same or higher rates. The paradox arose because women applied disproportionately to competitive departments.
COVID-19 mortality: In some early analyses, countries with more advanced healthcare appeared to have higher mortality rates. Within matched patient groups (controlling for age and severity), better healthcare reduced mortality. The aggregate correlation was driven by age confounds.
Wages across demographic groups: Total wages can fall for every group while rising in the aggregate, if people are shifting from lower-paid to higher-paid occupations (your slides show this exact example).

title: 17  Simpson’s Paradox
section: 18.3 The Lesson for Nested Data Analysis
text:
18.3 The Lesson for Nested Data Analysis
Simpson’s Paradox is not rare. It is the default when: 1. Groups differ in their average level of the predictor 2. Groups differ in their average level of the outcome 3. The predictor and outcome differ in sign between levels
Every time you ignore nesting, you risk a Simpson’s Paradox situation. The mixed-effects models in this workshop are partly a defense against this — they force you to separate within-group and between-group relationships explicitly.






ImportantThe Check



Before reporting any correlation or regression coefficient from nested data, ask: Does this relationship hold in the same direction within units as it does across units? If not, you are reporting a Simpson’s Paradox, not a real effect.

title: 18  Overview
text: Module 3: Inferential Threats & Identification

title: 18  Overview
text:
18.1 The “Independent” Variable
A variable only earns the label “independent” after you have done the work to establish that it is exogenously related to your outcome. Until then, it is just a predictor — one that may be deeply entangled with other forces that also shape the outcome.
Nested data creates special entanglement problems. When people are grouped into firms, schools, neighborhoods, or time periods, the things that determine their group membership are often the same things that affect their outcomes. A person does not end up in a high-autonomy firm at random. A student does not end up in a high-quality classroom at random. A country does not adopt a policy at random.
This module is about recognizing these entanglements — endogeneity — and learning the identification strategies that let us recover credible causal claims despite them.

title: 18  Overview
text:
18.2 What This Module Covers
Part 1: Endogeneity — The three sources of endogeneity in social science data: omitted variable bias, simultaneity/reverse causality, and selection. What each looks like in the DGP, and how to recognize them.
Part 2: Instrumental Variables — When you cannot randomize, an instrument can do some of the work. What makes a valid instrument, the two-stage procedure, and real examples from consumer and organizational research.
Part 3: Selection Effects & Attrition — The many ways observations end up in your dataset non-randomly: representativeness failures, self-selection, survivorship, attrition in longitudinal studies, and researcher-driven exclusions.
Part 4: Regression Discontinuity Design — When treatment assignment is based on a threshold, the continuity of potential outcomes around that threshold gives you a local quasi-experiment. Assumptions, tests, and worked examples.
Part 5: Difference-in-Differences — When some units receive a treatment and others do not, and you have pre- and post-treatment observations, DiD recovers causal effects under the parallel trends assumption.
Part 6: Propensity Scores & Matching — When treatment is observational but you can condition on all relevant confounders, matching and weighting approaches can reduce bias.

title: 18  Overview
text:
18.3 The Through-Line: Identification
Every method in this module is an identification strategy — a way to isolate variation in your predictor that is (approximately) exogenous, and use only that variation to estimate the effect of interest.
The math of each strategy is not the hard part. The hard part is the judgment: Does your instrument really satisfy the exclusion restriction? Are your parallel trends really plausible? Can you really claim you have measured all confounders?
This module teaches both the tools and the judgment.

title: 19  Endogeneity
text: 20 Endogeneity

title: 19  Endogeneity
text:
20.1 What Does “Independent” Really Mean?
In regression, we call X the “independent variable.” The name suggests that X is free of any influence — that it varies on its own, unrelated to the forces that also shape Y. In a well-executed randomized experiment, this is approximately true.
In observational data — and in most nested data — this assumption is almost never satisfied.
Endogeneity means that your predictor X is correlated with the error term in your regression. Some of the forces that drive X are the same forces that drive Y, beyond what your model accounts for. When this happens, your regression coefficient is not an estimate of the effect of X on Y. It is a blend of that effect and spurious associations created by shared forces.
There are three main sources of endogeneity.

title: 19  Endogeneity
text:
20.2 Source 1: Omitted Variable Bias (OVB)
A variable Z influences both X and Y, but you have not included Z in your model. The effect of Z on Y gets attributed to X.
Classic example: Ice cream sales (X) predict drowning deaths (Y). Not because ice cream causes drowning — but because summer heat (Z) increases both.


Code
n <- 500
Z <- rnorm(n, 70, 10)       # Temperature (the confounder)
X <- 0.3 * Z + rnorm(n, 0, 5)  # Ice cream sales (caused by heat)
Y <- 0.5 * Z + rnorm(n, 0, 8)  # Drowning deaths (caused by heat, NOT ice cream)
# True effect of X on Y = 0

df_ovb <- tibble(X, Y, Z)

m_biased  <- lm(Y ~ X, data = df_ovb)
m_correct <- lm(Y ~ X + Z, data = df_ovb)

bind_rows(
  tidy(m_biased)  |> filter(term == "X") |> mutate(model = "Omits Z (biased)"),
  tidy(m_correct) |> filter(term == "X") |> mutate(model = "Includes Z (correct)")
) |>
  select(model, estimate, std.error, p.value) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3, caption = "OVB: the ice cream effect disappears once we control for heat")



OVB: the ice cream effect disappears once we control for heat


model
estimate
std.error
p.value




Omits Z (biased)
0.543
0.067
0.000


Includes Z (correct)
-0.052
0.070
0.457





Without Z, the X coefficient is large and significant — even though the true effect is zero.
In nested data: OVB is especially insidious because the omitted variable often varies at a higher level. Studying whether neighborhood green space predicts depression while omitting neighborhood income (which predicts both) biases your estimate. The omitted variable is a neighborhood-level characteristic — exactly the kind of thing that clustering creates.






WarningAssumption: No Omitted Variables Correlated with Both X and Y



How to check: Draw a causal diagram. List every plausible cause of Y and ask: is any of these also correlated with X? You cannot fully test this from data — it requires substantive reasoning about the DGP.
What to do: Add the variable if measurable. If not measurable, consider IV, fixed effects, or DiD.

title: 19  Endogeneity
text:
20.3 Source 2: Simultaneity / Reverse Causality
X causes Y, but Y also causes X. The causal arrow runs in both directions, creating a feedback loop.
Example: Income and spending. Higher income causes more spending. But the desire to spend more can motivate people to seek higher income. Both directions are real. A cross-sectional regression picks up a mix of both.
Simultaneity is especially common in panel and longitudinal data, where today’s outcome can become tomorrow’s predictor — a structure naturally produced by the nested DGP of time.






WarningAssumption: No Reverse Causality



How to check: Ask whether Y at time \(t\) could plausibly influence X at time \(t\) or \(t+1\).
What to do: Use lagged predictors (\(X_{t-1}\) predicting \(Y_t\)). Use IV. Use cross-lagged panel models (Module 4, Part 6).

title: 19  Endogeneity
text:
20.4 Source 3: Selection Effects
The observations in your dataset are not a random sample. Something about who ends up in your data is related to both X and Y. This is covered in depth in Part 3.

title: 19  Endogeneity
text:
20.5 Pearl’s Backdoor Criterion
Judea Pearl’s framework gives us precise language for endogeneity. A backdoor path from X to Y is any path that flows through a common cause — a path that goes “backwards” into X from a confounder.


Code
dag_df <- tibble(
  node = c("Z\n(Confounder)", "X\n(Predictor)", "Y\n(Outcome)"),
  x    = c(2, 1, 3),
  y    = c(2, 1, 1)
)

ggplot(dag_df, aes(x = x, y = y, label = node)) +
  geom_point(size = 22, color = "#deebf7", shape = 21, fill = "#deebf7", stroke = 1.5) +
  geom_text(size = 3.5, fontface = "bold", color = "#1e3a5f") +
  annotate("segment", x = 1.8, xend = 1.2, y = 1.9, yend = 1.1,
           arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.3, "cm")), color = "#e63946", linewidth = 1.2) +
  annotate("segment", x = 2.2, xend = 2.8, y = 1.9, yend = 1.1,
           arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.3, "cm")), color = "#e63946", linewidth = 1.2) +
  annotate("segment", x = 1.2, xend = 2.8, y = 1.0, yend = 1.0,
           arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.3, "cm")), color = "#1e3a5f", linewidth = 1.5) +
  annotate("text", x = 2, y = 0.75, label = "Causal path of interest",
           color = "#1e3a5f", size = 3.2) +
  annotate("text", x = 1.5, y = 1.62, label = "Backdoor\npath", color = "#e63946", size = 3) +
  xlim(0.5, 3.5) + ylim(0.5, 2.5) +
  theme_void() +
  labs(title = "Backdoor path: Z creates a non-causal correlation between X and Y")









Blocking the backdoor is the goal of every identification strategy in this module — either by including Z in the model (when measurable), by using fixed effects to absorb all time-invariant confounds, or by finding an instrument that purges the contaminated variation in X.

title: 20  Instrumental Variables
text: 21 Instrumental Variables

title: 20  Instrumental Variables
text:
21.1 When You Cannot Randomize
The gold standard for causal inference is a randomized experiment. But often you cannot randomize. An instrument is a variable that gives you some of the benefits of randomization without actually randomizing. It shifts X exogenously — through a channel that does not itself affect Y.

title: 20  Instrumental Variables
text:
21.2 The Three Requirements






WarningAssumption 1: Relevance



What it means: Z must actually cause X to change. An instrument that does not predict the predictor is useless (and produces infinite standard errors).
How to check: Regress X on Z (the “first stage”). The F-statistic on Z must be large — the standard rule of thumb is F > 10. Below 10, the instrument is “weak” and estimates become unreliable.








WarningAssumption 2: Exclusion Restriction



What it means: Z affects Y only through X — not through any other path. This is the key untestable assumption. You cannot verify it from data alone — you can only argue for it from theory and domain knowledge.
How to scrutinize: Trace every possible path from Z to Y. Is there any path that does not go through X?








WarningAssumption 3: Independence (Exogeneity of the Instrument)



What it means: Z is as-good-as-randomly assigned — it is not itself caused by the confounders that affect X and Y.
How to check: Verify that Z is balanced across observable pre-treatment covariates.

title: 20  Instrumental Variables
text:
21.3 Two-Stage Least Squares (TSLS)
Stage 1: Regress X on Z. Extract fitted values \(\hat{X}\) — the exogenous component of X.
Stage 2: Regress Y on \(\hat{X}\). This is the IV causal estimate.


Code
n <- 800

# Confounders: fatigue and commute stress
fatigue        <- rnorm(n, 50, 10)
commute_stress <- rnorm(n, 40, 8)

# Instrument: daylight saving time shift (exogenous clock reset)
DST_week <- rbinom(n, 1, 0.5)

# Time of day: influenced by DST AND by fatigue/stress
time_of_day <- 12 + 1.5 * DST_week - 0.1 * fatigue + rnorm(n, 0, 2)

# Variety seeking: affected by time of day AND by confounders
# True causal effect of time_of_day = 0.4
variety_seeking <- 3 + 0.4 * time_of_day - 0.2 * fatigue -
                   0.15 * commute_stress + rnorm(n, 0, 3)

df_iv <- tibble(variety_seeking, time_of_day, DST_week, fatigue, commute_stress)

# First stage: check instrument strength
m_first <- lm(time_of_day ~ DST_week, data = df_iv)
cat("First stage F-statistic:", round(summary(m_first)$fstatistic[1], 1), "\n")


First stage F-statistic: 113.1 


Code
# Naive OLS
m_ols <- lm(variety_seeking ~ time_of_day, data = df_iv)

# IV via TSLS
m_iv <- ivreg(variety_seeking ~ time_of_day | DST_week, data = df_iv)

bind_rows(
  tidy(m_ols) |> filter(term == "time_of_day") |> mutate(model = "OLS (biased)"),
  tidy(m_iv)  |> filter(term == "time_of_day") |> mutate(model = "IV / TSLS")
) |>
  select(model, estimate, std.error, p.value) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "OLS vs. IV: effect of time-of-day on variety seeking (true = 0.4)")



OLS vs. IV: effect of time-of-day on variety seeking (true = 0.4)


model
estimate
std.error
p.value




OLS (biased)
0.753
0.057
0.000


IV / TSLS
0.550
0.163
0.001





The OLS estimate is inflated by the confounders. The IV estimate recovers the true causal effect by using only the DST-driven variation in time-of-day.

title: 20  Instrumental Variables
text:
21.4 The LATE Interpretation
The IV estimate is not the population-average causal effect. It is the Local Average Treatment Effect (LATE): the causal effect for compliers — units whose treatment status actually changed because of the instrument. People unaffected by DST (those who keep the same schedule regardless) are uninformative for the IV estimate.
This limits generalizability. Whether the LATE is the quantity you care about depends on your research question.

title: 20  Instrumental Variables
text:
21.5 Finding Good Instruments
The exclusion restriction is the bottleneck. Common instrument classes in social science:








Instrument class
Example
Why valid




Natural experiment
Lottery assignment
Random by design


Regulatory threshold
Age cutoff for program
Arbitrary threshold


Geographic variation
Distance to hospital
Not chosen by individual


Time-based shock
Policy change, DST
Exogenous timing


Peer assignment
Random roommate assignment
Administrative randomization



The math is easy. Finding a credible instrument — one whose exclusion restriction holds and can be argued for — requires deep domain knowledge and creative thinking about your DGP.

title: 21  Selection Effects & Attrition
text: 22 Selection Effects & Attrition

title: 21  Selection Effects & Attrition
section: 22.1 When Your Sample is Not Your Population
text:
22.1 When Your Sample is Not Your Population
Even with a perfect model, if your observations are not representative of the population you care about, your conclusions may be wrong. Selection effects are the family of processes by which observations non-randomly enter or leave your dataset.
Selection is especially dangerous in nested and longitudinal data because the same processes that structure your data also drive who stays in it over time.

title: 21  Selection Effects & Attrition
text:
22.2 Type 1: Representativeness / Undercover Bias
Your sampling frame does not cover the full population you want to generalize to.
Secondary data example: Online review platform data. People who leave reviews are not a random sample — they felt strongly enough to write one (typically either very satisfied or very dissatisfied). Analyses generalize to “opinionated reviewers,” not “all customers.”
Experimental example: Convenience samples from university subject pools (WEIRD populations — Western, Educated, Industrialized, Rich, Democratic) may not generalize to the adults whose behavior you are studying.






WarningAssumption: Sampling Frame Covers the Population of Interest



How to check: Compare your sample’s distributions on key variables to known population statistics. Are any groups systematically over- or under-represented?
What to do: Acknowledge the limitation. Use stratified sampling. Apply post-stratification weights if population distributions are known.

title: 21  Selection Effects & Attrition
text:
22.3 Type 2: Self-Selection / Non-Response Bias
Participants choose whether to enter your study based on factors related to your variables of interest.
The landmark example: the 1936 Literary Digest poll predicted Landon would defeat Roosevelt by a landslide, based on 2.4 million responses. Their sampling frame (telephone directories and car registration lists) over-represented wealthy Republicans. Roosevelt won in a historic landslide.


Code
n_total    <- 1000
health_con <- rnorm(n_total, 50, 10)  # latent health consciousness

# Health-conscious people more likely to participate
p_participate <- plogis(-2 + 0.06 * health_con)
participated  <- rbinom(n_total, 1, p_participate)

# Outcome influenced by health consciousness
health_score <- 40 + 0.5 * health_con + rnorm(n_total, 0, 8)

tibble(
  Group    = c("Full population", "Self-selected sample"),
  N        = c(n_total, sum(participated)),
  Mean_health = c(mean(health_score),
                   mean(health_score[participated == 1]))
) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 2, caption = "Self-selection inflates estimated health level")



Self-selection inflates estimated health level


Group
N
Mean_health




Full population
1000

64.96

title: 21  Selection Effects & Attrition
text:
22.4 Type 3: Survivorship Bias
You only observe units that survived some selection filter correlated with your outcome.
WWII aircraft: Analysts studied bullet-hole patterns on returning planes to decide where to add armor. Abraham Wald pointed out: planes hit in the zero-hole areas never returned. Those were the critical spots to armor.
In longitudinal research: Studying predictors of firm success using currently existing firms excludes all firms that failed. If failure is related to your predictors, estimates are systematically optimistic.






WarningAssumption: No Survivorship Bias



How to check: Ask whether your outcome is systematically related to the probability of appearing in your dataset. If yes, you likely have survivorship bias.
What to do: Obtain historical data including non-survivors. Use survival analysis (Module 6, Part 3) to model time-to-exit explicitly.

title: 21  Selection Effects & Attrition
section: 22.5 Type 4: Attrition Bias in Longitudinal Data
text:
22.5 Type 4: Attrition Bias in Longitudinal Data
Participants drop out of your study non-randomly over time. This is one of the most serious threats to longitudinal research — and a direct consequence of the nested DGP of time.


Code
n_people <- 200
n_waves  <- 5

baseline_dep <- rnorm(n_people, 50, 10)
treatment    <- rbinom(n_people, 1, 0.5)

df_long <- expand_grid(person = 1:n_people, wave = 1:n_waves) |>
  mutate(
    baseline  = baseline_dep[person],
    treat     = treatment[person],
    depression = baseline - 5 * treat * (wave / n_waves) + rnorm(n(), 0, 4)
  )

# People with HIGH depression are more likely to drop out
df_long <- df_long |>
  group_by(person) |>
  mutate(
    drop_risk   = plogis(-3 + 0.04 * mean(depression)),
    dropped_out = cummax(rbinom(n(), 1, drop_risk)),
    observed    = dropped_out == 0 | wave == 1
  ) |>
  ungroup()

complete_cases <- df_long |>
  group_by(person) |> filter(all(observed)) |> ungroup()

tibble(
  Sample = c("All (Wave 1)", "Completers only"),
  N      = c(n_distinct(df_long$person[df_long$wave == 1]),
              n_distinct(complete_cases$person)),
  Mean_baseline_depression = c(
    mean(baseline_dep),
    mean(unique(complete_cases$baseline))
  )
) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 2,
    caption = "Attrition: completers have lower baseline depression (healthier)")



Attrition: completers have lower baseline depression (healthier)

# True treatment effect = -5
m_completers <- lm(depression ~ treat + wave + baseline, data = complete_cases)
m_all        <- lm(depression ~ treat + wave + baseline,
                   data = df_long |> filter(observed))

bind_rows(
  broom::tidy(m_completers) |> filter(term == "treat") |>
    mutate(model = "Complete cases (attrition bias)"),
  broom::tidy(m_all) |> filter(term == "treat") |>
    mutate(model = "All observed data")
) |>
  select(model, estimate, std.error, p.value) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "Attrition biases the treatment effect toward zero (true = -5)")

WarningAssumption: Dropout Mechanism is Ignorable



MCAR: Dropout is unrelated to any variable. Very rare.
MAR: Dropout depends only on observed variables. If MAR holds, mixed-effects models using all available data (not just completers) give valid estimates.
MNAR: Dropout depends on the unobserved outcome itself. Requires sensitivity analysis or selection models (Heckman).
How to check: Compare baseline characteristics of completers vs. dropouts. If they differ substantially, dropout is non-random.

title: 21  Selection Effects & Attrition
text:
22.6 Type 5: Exclusion Bias
The researcher removes observations non-randomly — typically based on criteria correlated with the outcome.
The key principle: Any data deletion that is non-random creates selection bias. This includes removing “outliers” based on outcome values, excluding protocol violators after seeing results, or dropping countries/firms that seem “too different.”






TipPre-Registration as a Defense



Pre-registering exclusion criteria before data collection prevents post-hoc rationalization of exclusions that benefit your hypothesis. It does not eliminate the statistical bias, but it prevents the researcher from inadvertently introducing it.

title: 22  Regression Discontinuity Design
text: 23 Regression Discontinuity Design

title: 22  Regression Discontinuity Design
text:
23.1 The Threshold as a Quasi-Experiment
Suppose college admission is partly determined by a test score cutoff: applicants above 70 are admitted, those below are not. People just below 70 and just above 70 are otherwise very similar — nearly identical test scores, similar backgrounds, similar demographics. The only thing separating them is whether they fell above or below an arbitrary threshold.
This discontinuity in treatment assignment, driven by a continuous running variable, is the foundation of Regression Discontinuity Design (RDD).
The key insight: right at the threshold, treatment is as-good-as-randomly assigned, because small differences in the running variable near the cutoff are essentially random noise in the DGP.

title: 22  Regression Discontinuity Design
text:
23.2 The Core Assumption






WarningAssumption: Continuity of Potential Outcomes at the Threshold



What it means: In the absence of treatment, the expected outcome must be a continuous function of the running variable at the cutoff. The only reason outcomes should jump at the threshold is the treatment.
Why it fails: - Sorting/manipulation: If people can manipulate their running variable to cross the threshold (retaking tests, strategic reporting), those just above and just below are not comparable. - Other policies at the same cutoff: If another treatment also kicks in at exactly the same threshold, you cannot separate the effects. - Anticipation: If people change behavior in anticipation of crossing the threshold.
How to check: McCrary density test — a spike in the running variable just above the cutoff indicates manipulation.

title: 22  Regression Discontinuity Design
text:
23.3 A Worked Example


Code
n <- 1000
# Running variable: test score, centered at cutoff = 0
test_score <- rnorm(n, 0, 1)
admitted   <- as.integer(test_score >= 0)
# True causal effect of admission on earnings = 8
earnings   <- 50 + 5 * test_score + 8 * admitted + rnorm(n, 0, 5)

df_rdd <- tibble(test_score, admitted, earnings)

ggplot(df_rdd, aes(x = test_score, y = earnings, color = factor(admitted))) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.2, size = 1) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", formula = y ~ poly(x, 2), se = TRUE, linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "black", linewidth = 1) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#aec6e8", "#1e3a5f"),
                     labels = c("Not admitted", "Admitted")) +
  labs(title = "RDD: earnings vs. test score",
       subtitle = "Jump at the dashed cutoff = causal effect of admission",
       x = "Test Score (centered at cutoff)", y = "Earnings",
       color = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)











Code
rdd_result <- rdrobust(y = df_rdd$earnings, x = df_rdd$test_score, c = 0)
summary(rdd_result)

title: 22  Regression Discontinuity Design
text:
23.4 The Density Test


Code
ggplot(df_rdd, aes(x = test_score)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 40, fill = "#1e3a5f", color = "white", alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, color = "#e63946", linewidth = 1.2, linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(title = "Density test: check for manipulation at the cutoff",
       subtitle = "A spike just right of the dashed line = sorting violation",
       x = "Test Score (centered at cutoff)", y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

title: 22  Regression Discontinuity Design
text:
23.5 Falsification Tests
Run the RDD on pre-treatment covariates (age, gender, family income). These cannot be caused by the treatment. A jump at the threshold in a covariate means something else is discontinuous there — a serious threat to validity.


Code
df_rdd <- df_rdd |> mutate(age = 18 + rnorm(n, 0, 1))
fake   <- rdrobust(y = df_rdd$age, x = df_rdd$test_score, c = 0)
cat("Jump in age at cutoff (should be ~0):", round(fake$coef[1], 3),
    "| p =", round(fake$pv[1], 3), "\n")


Jump in age at cutoff (should be ~0): -0.02 | p = 0.915

title: 22  Regression Discontinuity Design
text:
23.6 Bandwidth Sensitivity
The estimate should be stable as you vary the bandwidth around the default. Dramatic changes signal dependence on functional form.


Code
bws  <- seq(0.3, 1.5, by = 0.2)
ests <- sapply(bws, \(bw)
  rdrobust(y = df_rdd$earnings, x = df_rdd$test_score, c = 0, h = bw)$coef[1])

tibble(Bandwidth = bws, Estimate = ests) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = Bandwidth, y = Estimate)) +
  geom_line(color = "#1e3a5f", linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(size = 3, color = "#1e3a5f") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 8, linetype = "dashed", color = "#e63946") +
  labs(title = "Bandwidth sensitivity (red = true effect)",
       x = "Bandwidth", y = "Estimated Discontinuity") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

title: 23  Difference-in-Differences
text: 24 Difference-in-Differences

title: 23  Difference-in-Differences
text:
24.1 The Idea
A policy rolls out: some firms must adopt a new management practice; others are unaffected. You have productivity data from before and after. Can you estimate the effect?
The naive approach — compare treated and control firms after the rollout — is biased if they were already different. The Difference-in-Differences (DiD) approach subtracts the control group’s change from the treated group’s change:
\[\hat{\tau}_{DiD} = \underbrace{(\bar{Y}_{T,post} - \bar{Y}_{T,pre})}_{\text{treated group change}} - \underbrace{(\bar{Y}_{C,post} - \bar{Y}_{C,pre})}_{\text{control group change}}\]
The control group’s change serves as the counterfactual: what would have happened to treated firms if they had not been treated?

title: 23  Difference-in-Differences
text:
24.2 The Core Assumption






WarningAssumption: Parallel Trends



What it means: In the absence of treatment, treated and control units would have followed the same trend over time. They can start at different levels — DiD handles that. But their trajectories must be parallel.
How to check: 1. Visual inspection: Plot pre-treatment trends for both groups. They should look parallel. 2. Formal pre-trend test: Regress the outcome on treat × time using only pre-treatment data. Significant interaction = non-parallel trends. 3. Event study plot: Estimate separate treatment effects for each period. Pre-period coefficients should be near zero.
Why it fails: Treated and control units may have been on different trajectories before treatment — perhaps because units that selected into treatment were already changing faster. No statistical fix can rescue DiD if parallel trends are implausible given the selection process.

title: 23  Difference-in-Differences
text:
24.3 A Simulated Example


Code
n_firms <- 80
n_waves <- 6  # waves 1-3: pre; 4-6: post

firm_treat   <- c(rep(1, 40), rep(0, 40))
firm_base    <- rnorm(n_firms, 50, 8)

df_did <- expand_grid(firm = 1:n_firms, wave = 1:n_waves) |>
  mutate(
    treat      = firm_treat[firm],
    post       = as.integer(wave > 3),
    baseline   = firm_base[firm],
    trend      = (wave - 3.5) * 1.5,
    te         = treat * post * 10,
    productivity = baseline + trend + te + rnorm(n(), 0, 4)
  )

# Plot parallel pre-trends
group_means <- df_did |>
  group_by(wave, treat) |>
  summarise(mean_prod = mean(productivity), .groups = "drop") |>
  mutate(group = ifelse(treat == 1, "Treated", "Control"))

ggplot(group_means, aes(x = wave, y = mean_prod, color = group, linetype = group)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.3) + geom_point(size = 3) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 3.5, linetype = "dotted", color = "gray40") +
  annotate("text", x = 3.65, y = max(group_means$mean_prod),
           label = "Treatment", hjust = 0, color = "gray40", size = 3.5) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#aec6e8", "#1e3a5f")) +
  labs(title = "DiD: parallel pre-trends, divergence post-treatment",
       x = "Wave", y = "Productivity", color = NULL, linetype = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

m_did <- lm(productivity ~ treat * post + baseline, data = df_did)
tidy(m_did) |>

title: 23  Difference-in-Differences
text:
24.6 Additional Assumptions






WarningAssumption: SUTVA — No Spillovers



What it means: Treatment of unit A should not affect the outcomes of unit B. If treated firms compete with control firms and treatment improves treated performance at control firms’ expense, the control trend is contaminated.
How to check: Test whether control units near treated units trend differently from distant controls.








WarningAssumption: Stable Composition



What it means: The composition of treated and control groups should not change differentially after treatment. If the policy causes the most productive employees to leave treated firms, the post-treatment group is a different population.
How to check: Check whether observable characteristics of units change differentially post-treatment.

title: 24  Propensity Scores & Matching
text: 25 Propensity Scores & Matching

title: 24  Propensity Scores & Matching
text:
25.1 The Observational Study Problem
You want to estimate whether participating in a corporate wellness program improves employee health, using observational HR data. Employees self-selected into the program — healthier, more motivated employees are more likely to participate. A naive comparison of participants vs. non-participants will overestimate the benefit.
Propensity score methods reduce this bias by creating a comparison group similar to the treated group on all observed confounders. They work by estimating the probability that each unit received treatment — the propensity score — then matching or weighting on that score.

title: 24  Propensity Scores & Matching
text:
25.2 The Propensity Score
\[e(X) = P(\text{Treatment} = 1 \mid X)\]
The probability of receiving treatment given observed covariates. Key result (Rosenbaum & Rubin, 1983): conditioning on \(e(X)\) is equivalent to conditioning on all of \(X\) — you do not need to match on each covariate separately.


Code
n <- 600
age           <- rnorm(n, 40, 10)
health_aware  <- rnorm(n, 50, 12)
job_stress    <- rnorm(n, 45, 8)

# Treatment probability driven by confounders
p_treat   <- plogis(-4 + 0.05 * age + 0.06 * health_aware - 0.03 * job_stress)
treatment <- rbinom(n, 1, p_treat)

# True ATE = 5
health_score <- 40 + 0.3 * age + 0.4 * health_aware - 0.2 * job_stress +
                5 * treatment + rnorm(n, 0, 6)

df_ps <- tibble(age, health_aware, job_stress, treatment, health_score)

m_naive <- lm(health_score ~ treatment, data = df_ps)
cat("Naive estimate:", round(coef(m_naive)["treatment"], 2),
    "— true effect = 5\n")


Naive estimate: 10.11 — true effect = 5

title: 24  Propensity Scores & Matching
section: 25.3 Step 1: Estimate Propensity Scores and Check Overlap
text:
25.3 Step 1: Estimate Propensity Scores and Check Overlap


Code
ps_model <- glm(treatment ~ age + health_aware + job_stress,
                family = binomial, data = df_ps)
df_ps <- df_ps |> mutate(ps = fitted(ps_model))

ggplot(df_ps, aes(x = ps, fill = factor(treatment))) +
  geom_histogram(alpha = 0.6, bins = 30, position = "identity") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("#aec6e8", "#1e3a5f"),
                    labels = c("Control", "Treated")) +
  labs(title = "Overlap: propensity score distributions",
       subtitle = "Both groups must cover the same PS range for valid comparison",
       x = "Propensity Score", y = "Count", fill = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)















WarningAssumption 1: Overlap (Common Support)



What it means: Every unit must have a propensity score strictly between 0 and 1. Units with PS near 0 or 1 have no valid comparison units — causal inference is not possible for them.
How to check: Plot the PS distributions for treated and control groups. Trim units outside the common support region.

This is the critical untestable assumption. Propensity score methods can only control for variables you measured. Any unobserved confounder still biases the estimate.
How to scrutinize: Conduct a sensitivity analysis (Rosenbaum bounds) asking: how large would an unmeasured confounder need to be to overturn your conclusion?

title: 24  Propensity Scores & Matching
section: 25.6 PSM vs. IPW vs. Doubly Robust
text:
25.6 PSM vs. IPW vs. Doubly Robust









Method
How it works
Key advantage
Key risk




PSM
Match treated to similar controls
Intuitive; easy to check balance
Discards unmatched units


IPW
Weight by inverse of propensity score
Uses all units
Extreme weights for PS near 0 or 1


Doubly robust
Combines PS model + outcome model
Consistent if either model is correct
Slightly more complex



Doubly robust estimation provides an extra layer of protection: your estimate is consistent if either the propensity model or the outcome model is correctly specified — not necessarily both.

title: 25  Overview
text: Module 4: Longitudinal Data & Change

title: 25  Overview
section: 25.1 Time is the Ultimate Nesting Variable
text:
25.1 Time is the Ultimate Nesting Variable
Every person, firm, country, or organism exists within a stream of time. When you measure them repeatedly, your observations are nested within that stream. Each measurement is a snapshot of an ongoing trajectory — the DGP is a time-varying process, and your data is a discrete sample from it.
This creates two profound challenges for analysis:

Observations within a person are correlated. Your mood at 9am is related to your mood at 10am. A firm’s revenue this quarter predicts its revenue next quarter. Treating these as independent observations — as standard regression does — violates independence and inflates Type I error.
Change itself is the phenomenon. In many longitudinal studies, the question is not just “what is the average level?” but “how does this change over time, and does it change differently for different people?” A model that treats all observations as exchangeable — ignoring that some come first and some come later — cannot answer this question.

Module 4 builds the toolkit for modeling change over time correctly.

title: 25  Overview
text:
25.2 What This Module Covers
Part 1: From RM-ANOVA to Mixed Models — The repeated measures ANOVA that most psychologists learned makes a specific assumption about the covariance structure of repeated observations (compound symmetry/sphericity). We show concretely what this assumption means, why it almost always fails, and how mixed-effects models relax it.
Part 2: Covariance Structures — The covariance matrix of your repeated observations is not just a nuisance to be controlled — it is a window into the DGP. We catalog the main structures (compound symmetry, AR(1), unstructured, Toeplitz), show what each implies about how measurements relate over time, and explain how to choose among them.
Part 3: Growth Curve Models — When the question is how outcomes change over time and whether trajectories differ across people, growth curve models are the right tool. We build from simple linear growth to random slopes, nonlinear trajectories, and predictors of growth.
Part 4: Latent Growth Curve Models in SEM — The same growth curve model can be estimated in a structural equation modeling (SEM) framework. We show the connection, explain when the SEM approach adds value (modeling measurement error, multiple growth processes), and demonstrate the equivalence using lavaan.
Part 5: Autoregressive Structures — When today’s outcome predicts tomorrow’s, the covariance structure is autoregressive. We explain AR(1) models, how to specify them in nlme, and the assumptions of stationarity.
Part 6: Cross-Lagged Panel Models — When you want to understand the dynamic relationship between two variables over time — does A cause B, does B cause A, or do they mutually reinforce each other — cross-lagged panel models (and their improved version, the RI-CLPM) provide the framework.

title: 26  From RM-ANOVA to Mixed Models
text: 27 From RM-ANOVA to Mixed Models
crumbs: 26  From RM-ANOVA to Mixed Models

title: 26  From RM-ANOVA to Mixed Models
text:
27.1 The Standard Psychology Approach
Repeated measures ANOVA (RM-ANOVA) has been the default tool for within-subject designs in psychology for decades. It is familiar, built into every statistics textbook, and available in SPSS, JASP, and R. And it makes an assumption about your data that is almost always wrong.
That assumption is sphericity.
crumbs: 26  From RM-ANOVA to Mixed Models

title: 26  From RM-ANOVA to Mixed Models
text:
27.2 What is Sphericity?
Sphericity is a constraint on the variance-covariance matrix of your repeated measurements. Specifically, it requires that the variances of all pairwise differences between time points are equal.
Imagine you measure anxiety at three time points: before a speech, immediately after, and one week later. Sphericity requires that: - Var(Time1 − Time2) = Var(Time1 − Time3) = Var(Time2 − Time3)
In plain language: the variability in how much people change is the same regardless of which two time points you compare. This is equivalent to assuming compound symmetry — that all time points correlate equally with each other.


Code
# Visualize compound symmetry vs. a realistic AR(1) structure
library(Matrix)

# Compound symmetry: equal variances, equal covariances
CS <- matrix(c(1.0, 0.5, 0.5, 0.5,
               0.5, 1.0, 0.5, 0.5,
               0.5, 0.5, 1.0, 0.5,
               0.5, 0.5, 0.5, 1.0), 4, 4)

# AR(1): closer time points are more correlated
rho <- 0.7
AR1 <- outer(1:4, 1:4, FUN = function(i, j) rho^abs(i - j))

# Unstructured: no constraints
US <- matrix(c(1.0, 0.8, 0.6, 0.3,
               0.8, 1.0, 0.7, 0.5,
               0.6, 0.7, 1.0, 0.8,
               0.3, 0.5, 0.8, 1.0), 4, 4)

plot_cov <- function(M, title) {
  as.data.frame(M) |>
    rownames_to_column("row") |>
    pivot_longer(-row, names_to = "col", values_to = "corr") |>
    mutate(row = factor(row, levels = rev(unique(row))),
           col = factor(col)) |>
    ggplot(aes(x = col, y = row, fill = corr)) +
    geom_tile(color = "white") +
    geom_text(aes(label = round(corr, 1)), size = 4) +
    scale_fill_gradient2(low = "#f7fbff", mid = "#6baed6", high = "#08306b",
                         midpoint = 0.5, limits = c(0, 1)) +
    labs(title = title, x = "Time point", y = "Time point", fill = "Corr") +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
    theme(legend.position = "none")
}

library(patchwork)
plot_cov(CS, "Compound Symmetry\n(sphericity assumption)") +
plot_cov(AR1, "AR(1)\n(more realistic)") +
plot_cov(US, "Unstructured\n(no constraints)")









The compound symmetry structure (left) forces all off-diagonal entries to be equal. The AR(1) structure (middle) lets correlations decay with time lag — closer time points are more correlated. The unstructured matrix (right) lets each correlation be whatever the data say.
Real longitudinal data almost always looks more like AR(1) or unstructured than compound symmetry.
crumbs: 26  From RM-ANOVA to Mixed Models

title: 26  From RM-ANOVA to Mixed Models
section: 27.3 What Happens When Sphericity is Violated?
text:
27.3 What Happens When Sphericity is Violated?
When sphericity fails, RM-ANOVA F-tests are anti-conservative — Type I error rates are inflated. You reject the null hypothesis more often than your alpha level would suggest.


Code
# Simulate data that violates sphericity: correlations decay with lag
simulate_repeated <- function(n = 50, n_times = 4, spherical = TRUE) {
  if (spherical) {
    # Compound symmetry (sphericity holds)
    Sigma <- CS * 4  # multiply by variance
  } else {
    # AR(1) (sphericity violated)
    Sigma <- AR1 * 4
  }

  # Simulate null data (no treatment effect) and check Type I error
  MASS::mvrnorm(n, mu = rep(0, n_times), Sigma = Sigma)
}

# Under the null (no effect), how often does RM-ANOVA reject?
n_sims <- 1000
type1_spherical <- numeric(n_sims)
type1_violated  <- numeric(n_sims)

for (i in 1:n_sims) {
  y_sph <- simulate_repeated(spherical = TRUE)
  y_vio <- simulate_repeated(spherical = FALSE)

  # Reshape and run RM-ANOVA
  extract_pval <- function(y_mat) {
    df_aov <- data.frame(
      Y      = c(y_mat),
      time   = factor(rep(1:4, each = 50)),
      person = factor(rep(1:50, 4))
    )
    aov_fit <- aov(Y ~ time + Error(person/time), data = df_aov)
    s <- summary(aov_fit)
    # Extract p-value from the within-person time effect
    tryCatch(s[["Error: person:time"]][[1]][["Pr(>F)"]][1],
             error = function(e) NA_real_)
  }
  pval_sph <- extract_pval(y_sph)
  pval_vio <- extract_pval(y_vio)

  type1_spherical[i] <- ifelse(is.null(pval_sph), NA, pval_sph < 0.05)
  type1_violated[i]  <- ifelse(is.null(pval_vio), NA, pval_vio < 0.05)
}

tibble(
  Condition = c("Sphericity holds (CS)", "Sphericity violated (AR1)"),
  `Type I Error Rate` = c(mean(type1_spherical, na.rm = TRUE),
                           mean(type1_violated,  na.rm = TRUE))
) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "Type I error under sphericity vs. violation (nominal alpha = 0.05)")



Type I error under sphericity vs. violation (nominal alpha = 0.05)


Condition
Type I Error Rate




Sphericity holds (CS)
0.038


Sphericity violated (AR1)
0.062











WarningAssumption: Sphericity (RM-ANOVA Only)



What it means: All pairwise variance differences among time points are equal. Equivalent to compound symmetry in the covariance matrix.
Mauchly’s test: The standard test for sphericity in RM-ANOVA. If Mauchly’s W is significant (p < 0.05), sphericity is violated.
The problem with Mauchly’s test: It is sensitive to sample size (underpowered in small samples; overpowered in large ones). It also tests an exact assumption — the question is not whether sphericity is exactly true but whether the violation is large enough to matter.
Corrections within RM-ANOVA: Greenhouse-Geisser or Huynh-Feldt corrections adjust the degrees of freedom. These are better than nothing but still assume a specific covariance structure.
The better solution: Switch to mixed-effects models, which do not assume sphericity and let you specify the appropriate covariance structure for your data.
crumbs: 26  From RM-ANOVA to Mixed Models

title: 26  From RM-ANOVA to Mixed Models
text:
27.4 Mixed-Effects Models: No Sphericity Needed
A mixed-effects model makes no assumption about the overall shape of the covariance matrix (in its most general form). The random effects structure and the residual covariance structure together determine what the model implies about how measurements co-vary.


Code
# Simulate anxiety study: 3 time points, AR(1) correlation structure
n_people <- 60
n_times  <- 3
Sigma_ar <- AR1[1:3, 1:3] * 4  # true covariance is AR(1)

# No treatment effect (null data)
anx_data <- MASS::mvrnorm(n_people, mu = c(50, 50, 50), Sigma = Sigma_ar) |>
  as.data.frame() |>
  setNames(c("time1", "time2", "time3")) |>
  mutate(person = 1:n_people) |>
  pivot_longer(-person, names_to = "time", values_to = "anxiety") |>
  mutate(
    time_num = as.integer(str_extract(time, "[0-9]")),
    person   = factor(person)
  )

# RM-ANOVA
m_anova <- aov(anxiety ~ factor(time_num) + Error(person / factor(time_num)),
               data = anx_data)

# Mixed-effects model with AR(1) covariance
m_mlm <- lme(anxiety ~ factor(time_num),
             random = ~1 | person,
             correlation = corAR1(),
             data = anx_data)

cat("RM-ANOVA p-value for time:", round(summary(m_anova)$`Error: person:factor(time_num)`[[1]]["factor(time_num)", "Pr(>F)"], 4), "\n")


RM-ANOVA p-value for time: 0.1692 


Code
cat("MLM p-value for time:", round(anova(m_mlm)["factor(time_num)", "p-value"], 4), "\n")


MLM p-value for time: 0.2214 


When the true covariance structure is AR(1) (not compound symmetry), the mixed-effects model gives more accurate p-values because it uses the correct covariance model. RM-ANOVA’s p-value may be misleading.
crumbs: 26  From RM-ANOVA to Mixed Models

title: 26  From RM-ANOVA to Mixed Models
section: 27.5 The Key Advantages of Mixed Models Over RM-ANOVA
text:
27.5 The Key Advantages of Mixed Models Over RM-ANOVA








Feature
RM-ANOVA
Mixed-Effects Model

Assumes sphericity?

No

No

The only reason to prefer RM-ANOVA is familiarity. Mixed-effects models strictly dominate for any longitudinal design.
