title: 27  Covariance Structures
text: 28 Covariance Structures

title: 27  Covariance Structures
section: 28.1 The Covariance Matrix is a Scientific Statement
text:
28.1 The Covariance Matrix is a Scientific Statement
When you measure the same person at multiple time points, those measurements co-vary. How they co-vary is not a statistical nuisance — it is a substantive statement about the DGP. The choice of covariance structure encodes an assumption about how the underlying process operates over time.
The covariance structure specifies two things: variances (how variable are observations at each time point?) and covariances (how correlated are observations at different time points?).

title: 27  Covariance Structures
text:
28.2 The Main Structures

28.2.1 1. Compound Symmetry (CS)
All variances are equal. All covariances are equal regardless of lag.
\[\Sigma_{CS} = \begin{pmatrix} \sigma^2 & \sigma^2\rho & \sigma^2\rho \\ \sigma^2\rho & \sigma^2 & \sigma^2\rho \\ \sigma^2\rho & \sigma^2\rho & \sigma^2 \end{pmatrix}\]
When it makes sense: When there is no reason to think observations closer in time are more similar than distant observations. Very rare in practice.
When it fails: Almost always. Time 1 and Time 2 are almost always more correlated than Time 1 and Time 6.


28.2.2 2. Autoregressive Order 1 — AR(1)
Correlations decay exponentially with time lag. Observations one step apart correlate \(\rho\); two steps apart, \(\rho^2\); \(k\) steps apart, \(\rho^k\).
\[\Sigma_{AR1} = \sigma^2\begin{pmatrix} 1 & \rho & \rho^2 \\ \rho & 1 & \rho \\ \rho^2 & \rho & 1 \end{pmatrix}\]
When it makes sense: For many psychological, physiological, and economic time series where the past predicts the present and the influence fades over time. Very commonly appropriate.


28.2.3 3. Unstructured (UN)
No constraints. Every variance and covariance is estimated freely.
When it makes sense: When you have no prior beliefs about the pattern and enough data to estimate all parameters.
When it fails: With many time points or small samples, the unstructured model is overparameterized and produces unstable estimates.


28.2.4 4. Toeplitz
Like AR(1) but relaxes the constraint that correlations must follow an exponential decay — each lag has its own correlation, but the correlation only depends on the lag (not the absolute time point). A flexible middle ground.

title: 27  Covariance Structures
text:
28.3 Visualizing the Structures


Code
make_cov_plot <- function(Sigma, title) {
  n <- nrow(Sigma)
  # Convert to correlation
  D <- diag(1 / sqrt(diag(Sigma)))
  Corr <- D %*% Sigma %*% D

  data.frame(Corr) |>
    setNames(paste0("T", 1:n)) |>
    mutate(row = paste0("T", 1:n)) |>
    pivot_longer(-row, names_to = "col", values_to = "r") |>
    mutate(row = factor(row, levels = rev(paste0("T", 1:n))),
           col = factor(col, levels = paste0("T", 1:n))) |>
    ggplot(aes(x = col, y = row, fill = r)) +
    geom_tile(color = "white", linewidth = 1) +
    geom_text(aes(label = round(r, 2)), size = 3.5) +
    scale_fill_gradient2(low = "#f7fbff", mid = "#4292c6", high = "#08306b",
                         midpoint = 0.5, limits = c(-0.1, 1)) +
    labs(title = title, x = NULL, y = NULL) +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
    theme(legend.position = "none")
}

n <- 5
rho <- 0.6

CS   <- matrix(rho, n, n); diag(CS) <- 1; CS <- CS * 4
AR1  <- outer(0:(n-1), 0:(n-1), function(i, j) rho^abs(i-j)) * 4
Toep <- outer(0:(n-1), 0:(n-1), function(i, j) {
  c(1, 0.7, 0.5, 0.3, 0.2)[abs(i-j)+1]
}) * 4
UN <- matrix(c(4.0, 2.4, 1.8, 1.4, 1.0,
               2.4, 4.2, 2.2, 1.6, 1.1,
               1.8, 2.2, 4.5, 2.4, 1.5,
               1.4, 1.6, 2.4, 4.8, 2.2,
               1.0, 1.1, 1.5, 2.2, 5.0), n, n)

library(patchwork)
(make_cov_plot(CS,   "Compound Symmetry") +
 make_cov_plot(AR1,  "AR(1)")) /
(make_cov_plot(Toep, "Toeplitz") +
 make_cov_plot(UN,   "Unstructured"))

title: 27  Covariance Structures
text:
28.4 Specifying Covariance Structures in R
lme4 does not support custom covariance structures beyond the random effects structure. For explicit covariance modeling, use nlme:


Code
# Simulate data with AR(1) structure
n_people <- 50
n_times  <- 5
rho_true <- 0.7

Y_ar1 <- MASS::mvrnorm(n_people, rep(50, n_times),
                        outer(0:(n_times-1), 0:(n_times-1),
                              function(i,j) rho_true^abs(i-j)) * 9)

df_long <- as.data.frame(Y_ar1) |>
  setNames(paste0("t", 1:n_times)) |>
  mutate(person = 1:n_people) |>
  pivot_longer(-person, names_to = "time", values_to = "Y") |>
  mutate(time_num = as.integer(str_extract(time, "[0-9]")),
         person   = factor(person))

# Fit four competing models
m_cs  <- gls(Y ~ factor(time_num), correlation = corCompSymm(form = ~1 | person), data = df_long)
m_ar1 <- gls(Y ~ factor(time_num), correlation = corAR1(form = ~time_num | person), data = df_long)
m_un  <- gls(Y ~ factor(time_num), correlation = corSymm(form = ~time_num | person),
             weights = varIdent(form = ~1 | factor(time_num)), data = df_long)

# Compare AIC
tibble(
  Model = c("Compound Symmetry", "AR(1)", "Unstructured"),
  AIC   = c(AIC(m_cs), AIC(m_ar1), AIC(m_un)),
  BIC   = c(BIC(m_cs), BIC(m_ar1), BIC(m_un))
) |>
  arrange(AIC) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 1,
    caption = "Model comparison: AR(1) fits better than CS when data has AR structure")

title: 27  Covariance Structures
section: 28.5 Assumptions and How to Check Each Structure
text:
28.5 Assumptions and How to Check Each Structure






WarningAR(1) Assumption: Stationarity



What it means: The mean and variance of observations are constant over time, and the correlation between observations depends only on the time lag, not on the absolute time point.
How to check: Plot the mean and variance of your outcome at each time point. If either is trending systematically, stationarity is violated. A detrended model (with time as a fixed effect) may restore stationarity of the residuals.








WarningUnstructured Assumption: Sufficient Sample Size



What it means: The unstructured model estimates \(T(T+1)/2\) covariance parameters (for \(T\) time points). With 5 time points, that is 15 parameters just for the covariance structure.
How to check: Compare the number of observations to the number of covariance parameters. As a rough guide, you want at least 10× as many observations as parameters.

title: 27  Covariance Structures
text:
28.6 Model Selection Strategy
Use AIC/BIC to compare competing covariance structures:

Start with the AR(1) model as a reasonable default
Compare to compound symmetry (more constrained) and Toeplitz (more flexible)
Try unstructured if sample size allows
Choose the model with the lowest AIC, subject to convergence and interpretability

title: 28  Growth Curve Models
text: 29 Growth Curve Models

title: 28  Growth Curve Models
text:
29.1 Trajectories as the Target
Standard repeated measures models ask: “Is the mean outcome different across time points?” Growth curve models ask something richer: “How does each unit’s trajectory unfold over time, and what explains variation in those trajectories?”
The shift in framing is fundamental. We are not trying to estimate one average change — we are trying to recover the distribution of individual change processes that generated the data. Each person has their own DGP; growth curve models estimate the parameters of that distribution.

title: 28  Growth Curve Models
text:
29.2 The Unconditional Growth Model
Start with no predictors. Just time and people.


Code
n_people <- 60
n_times  <- 6  # weeks

# Each person has a random baseline (intercept) and random rate of change (slope)
person_intercepts <- rnorm(n_people, 50, 8)
person_slopes     <- rnorm(n_people, -2, 1.5)  # average decline of 2/week

df_growth <- expand_grid(person = 1:n_people, week = 0:(n_times-1)) |>
  mutate(
    stress = person_intercepts[person] +
             person_slopes[person] * week +
             rnorm(n(), 0, 4),
    person = factor(person)
  )

# Plot individual trajectories
sample_people <- sample(levels(df_growth$person), 12)

ggplot(df_growth |> filter(person %in% sample_people),
       aes(x = week, y = stress, group = person, color = person)) +
  geom_line(alpha = 0.7) + geom_point(size = 1.5, alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_smooth(data = df_growth, aes(group = NULL, color = NULL),
              method = "lm", color = "black", linewidth = 1.5, se = TRUE) +
  scale_color_viridis_d() +
  labs(title = "Growth curve: individual stress trajectories over 6 weeks",
       subtitle = "Colored lines = 12 individuals; black line = average trajectory",
       x = "Week", y = "Stress (0-100)") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")











Code
# Unconditional growth model
m_growth <- lmer(stress ~ week + (week | person), data = df_growth)
summary(m_growth)

title: 28  Growth Curve Models

29.3 Adding Predictors of Growth
The power of growth curve models comes from asking: what predicts the trajectory? We can add level-2 predictors to explain why some people’s intercepts and slopes differ from others.

df_growth <- df_growth |>
  mutate(treatment = rep(rbinom(n_people, 1, 0.5), each = n_times))

# Treatment might affect both initial level and rate of change
m_growth_pred <- lmer(stress ~ week * treatment + (week | person),
                      data = df_growth)

title: 28  Growth Curve Models
text:
29.4 Nonlinear Growth
Some processes do not change linearly. Symptoms might improve rapidly at first, then plateau. Performance curves often show diminishing returns. The growth curve framework accommodates nonlinearity.


Code
# Quadratic growth
df_growth_q <- expand_grid(person = 1:n_people, week = 0:8) |>
  mutate(
    person_int    = rep(rnorm(n_people, 50, 8), each = 9),
    person_lin    = rep(rnorm(n_people, -5, 2), each = 9),
    person_quad   = rep(rnorm(n_people, 0.5, 0.3), each = 9),
    stress        = person_int + person_lin * week + person_quad * week^2 + rnorm(n(), 0, 3),
    person        = factor(person)
  )

# Fit with quadratic time
m_quad <- lmer(stress ~ week + I(week^2) + (week | person), data = df_growth_q)

# Plot average predicted trajectory
df_growth_q <- df_growth_q |>
  mutate(fitted_stress = fitted(m_quad))

df_growth_q |>
  group_by(week) |>
  summarise(mean_obs = mean(stress), mean_fit = mean(fitted_stress)) |>
  pivot_longer(-week) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = week, y = value, color = name, linetype = name)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) + geom_point(size = 2.5) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#aec6e8", "#1e3a5f"),
                     labels = c("Fitted (quadratic)", "Observed")) +
  scale_linetype_manual(values = c("solid", "dashed"),
                        labels = c("Fitted (quadratic)", "Observed")) +
  labs(title = "Quadratic growth: curve captures the deceleration",
       x = "Week", y = "Mean Stress", color = NULL, linetype = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)















WarningAssumption: The Growth Function is Correctly Specified



What it means: Linear growth assumes constant rate of change. Quadratic growth assumes a parabolic trajectory. If the true trajectory is neither — e.g., a logarithmic curve or piecewise linear — your model will be misspecified.
How to check: Plot the observed means by time point. Does the pattern look linear? Is there a clear deceleration or acceleration that requires a curve? Use AIC to compare linear vs. quadratic models.








WarningAssumption: Random Slope for Time is Correctly Specified



What it means: Allowing only a random intercept (but not slope) for time assumes everyone changes at exactly the same rate. This is almost always wrong for developmental or therapeutic outcomes.
How to check: Compare the random-intercept-only model to the random-slope model using a likelihood ratio test. Plot individual trajectories — if they clearly fan out or diverge, a random slope is needed.

title: 29  Latent Growth Curve Models (SEM)
text: 30 Latent Growth Curve Models

title: 29  Latent Growth Curve Models (SEM)
section: 30.1 Two Languages for the Same Model
text:
30.1 Two Languages for the Same Model
The growth curve model from Part 3 and the Latent Growth Curve Model (LGC) from structural equation modeling are mathematically equivalent. They answer the same questions using different parameterizations and different software.
Understanding both languages is valuable because: 1. Many psychology researchers encounter LGC models in SEM training (Mplus, lavaan) before they encounter MLM-based growth models 2. The SEM framing makes certain things explicit that are implicit in MLM — particularly the role of measurement error and the ability to model multiple growth processes simultaneously 3. Reviewing both frames deepens understanding of what the model is actually doing

title: 29  Latent Growth Curve Models (SEM)
text:
30.2 The SEM Framing
In the latent growth curve model, the trajectory is represented by two latent variables: - Intercept factor (η_i): The latent initial level for each person - Slope factor (η_s): The latent rate of change for each person
Observed scores at each time point are modeled as: \[Y_{ti} = 1 \cdot \eta_i + \lambda_t \cdot \eta_s + \varepsilon_{ti}\]
where \(\lambda_t\) are factor loadings that encode the time metric (e.g., \(\lambda_1 = 0, \lambda_2 = 1, \lambda_3 = 2\) for linear growth), and \(\varepsilon_{ti}\) are measurement residuals.
The intercept factor has loadings of 1.0 at all time points. The slope factor has loadings equal to the time coding.

title: 29  Latent Growth Curve Models (SEM)
section: 30.3 Fitting an LGC Model in lavaan
text:
30.3 Fitting an LGC Model in lavaan


Code
# Simulate data
n <- 150
intercepts <- rnorm(n, 50, 8)
slopes     <- rnorm(n, -2, 1.5)

Y <- sapply(0:4, function(t) intercepts + slopes * t + rnorm(n, 0, 4))
colnames(Y) <- paste0("y", 1:5)
df_lgc <- as.data.frame(Y)




Code
# Latent growth curve model in lavaan
lgc_model <- '
  # Intercept factor (loadings all fixed to 1)
  intercept =~ 1*y1 + 1*y2 + 1*y3 + 1*y4 + 1*y5

  # Slope factor (loadings = time coding: 0, 1, 2, 3, 4)
  slope     =~ 0*y1 + 1*y2 + 2*y3 + 3*y4 + 4*y5

  # Means of latent factors (the fixed effects)
  intercept ~ 1
  slope     ~ 1

  # Variances of latent factors (random effects variance)
  intercept ~~ intercept
  slope     ~~ slope

  # Covariance between intercept and slope
  intercept ~~ slope

  # Residual variances (constrained equal across time for simplicity)
  y1 ~~ a*y1
  y2 ~~ a*y2
  y3 ~~ a*y3
  y4 ~~ a*y4
  y5 ~~ a*y5
'

m_lgc <- growth(lgc_model, data = df_lgc)
summary(m_lgc, fit.measures = TRUE)

title: 29  Latent Growth Curve Models (SEM)
section: 30.5 When the SEM Framing Adds Value
text:
30.5 When the SEM Framing Adds Value






NoteAdvantage 1: Modeling Measurement Error



In the SEM framework, each observed score \(Y_{ti}\) has a residual \(\varepsilon_{ti}\) that can be interpreted as measurement error — the gap between the true latent trajectory and the imperfect observed score. This is explicit in the LGC model.
In standard MLM, the residual is treated as a mix of measurement error and genuine within-person fluctuation. If your outcome is a measured construct (scale scores, ratings, test scores), the SEM framing forces you to be explicit about measurement.








NoteAdvantage 2: Multiple Growth Processes



With SEM, you can model two interrelated growth curves simultaneously — for instance, how anxiety and depression co-evolve over therapy. The covariance between the two intercept factors and between the two slope factors captures how initial levels and rates of change co-relate.
This is difficult to do cleanly in standard MLM.

title: 29  Latent Growth Curve Models (SEM)
text:
30.6 Assumptions of LGC Models






WarningAssumption: Correctly Specified Factor Loadings



What it means: The factor loadings for the slope factor encode the time metric. Linear loadings (0, 1, 2, 3…) assume linear growth. If growth is nonlinear, you can free some loadings to be estimated from the data — but the intercept must still be defined by fixing at least two loadings.
How to check: Test alternative loading patterns (free vs. fixed) and compare model fit using CFI, RMSEA, and SRMR.








WarningAssumption: Multivariate Normality



What it means: SEM estimation (default ML) assumes the observed variables are multivariate normal. Severely skewed outcomes or floor/ceiling effects can bias estimates.
How to check: Check univariate skewness and kurtosis. Use robust ML estimators (MLR in lavaan) if normality is questionable.

title: 30  Autoregressive Structures
text: 31 Autoregressive Structures

title: 30  Autoregressive Structures
text:
31.1 The Memory of Time
Some outcomes carry their history with them. Mood at 9am predicts mood at 10am. Anxiety on Monday affects anxiety on Tuesday. GDP in quarter 1 predicts GDP in quarter 2. This temporal dependency — the “memory” of the process — is not just nuisance variance. It is a feature of the DGP that your model must account for.
When you ignore serial dependence and treat residuals as independent, your standard errors are biased and your inference is wrong. More importantly, you are misrepresenting the process: you are claiming the outcome at each time point arises fresh, unconnected from what came before.

title: 30  Autoregressive Structures
text:
31.2 What AR(1) Means
In an autoregressive order 1 (AR(1)) process, each observation is a linear function of the immediately preceding observation plus random noise:
\[Y_t = \phi \cdot Y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t, \quad \varepsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2_\varepsilon)\]
The parameter \(\phi\) is the autoregressive coefficient — how strongly the past predicts the present. When \(\phi = 0\), observations are independent. When \(\phi = 0.9\), yesterday’s value strongly predicts today’s.


Code
# Simulate AR(1) process for one person over 30 days
phi   <- 0.75
sigma <- 3
n_t   <- 30

Y <- numeric(n_t)
Y[1] <- rnorm(1, 50, 5)
for (t in 2:n_t) Y[t] <- phi * Y[t-1] + rnorm(1, 0, sigma)

p1 <- tibble(t = 1:n_t, Y = Y) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = t, y = Y)) +
  geom_line(color = "#1e3a5f", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(size = 2, color = "#1e3a5f", alpha = 0.7) +
  labs(title = paste0("AR(1) process: φ = ", phi),
       subtitle = "Each value depends on the previous",
       x = "Day", y = "Outcome") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

# ACF plot
acf_vals <- acf(Y, plot = FALSE, lag.max = 10)
p2 <- tibble(lag = 0:10, acf = acf_vals$acf[,1,1]) |>
  filter(lag > 0) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = lag, y = acf)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#1e3a5f", alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, color = "black") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = c(-1, 1) * 1.96 / sqrt(n_t),
             linetype = "dashed", color = "#e63946") +
  labs(title = "Autocorrelation function (ACF)",
       subtitle = "Should decay exponentially for AR(1)",
       x = "Lag", y = "ACF") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

library(patchwork)
p1 + p2

title: 30  Autoregressive Structures
text:
31.5 The Assumptions of AR(1) Models






WarningAssumption 1: Stationarity



What it means: The mean and variance of the process are constant over time, and the autocorrelation depends only on the lag, not the absolute time point. An AR(1) process is stationary when \(|\phi| < 1\).
How to check: Plot the mean and variance of your outcome at each time point. Systematic trends violate stationarity. Fit a detrended model (including time as a fixed effect) and check whether residuals are stationary.
What to do if violated: Include time trends as fixed effects. Consider differencing the outcome (analyzing change scores rather than raw scores).








WarningAssumption 2: Only Lag-1 Dependence



What it means: AR(1) assumes that \(Y_{t-2}\) predicts \(Y_t\) only through \(Y_{t-1}\) — once you condition on yesterday, the day before yesterday adds no additional predictive power.
How to check: Examine the partial autocorrelation function (PACF). If the PACF shows significant values at lag 2 or beyond, you need AR(2) or a higher-order model.


Code
pacf_vals <- pacf(Y, plot = FALSE, lag.max = 10)
tibble(lag = 1:10, pacf = pacf_vals$acf[,1,1]) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = lag, y = pacf)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#1e3a5f", alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = c(-1, 1) * 1.96 / sqrt(n_t),
             linetype = "dashed", color = "#e63946") +
  labs(title = "Partial ACF: only lag 1 significant = AR(1) is sufficient",
       x = "Lag", y = "Partial ACF") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

title: 31  Cross-Lagged Panel Models
text: 32 Cross-Lagged Panel Models

title: 31  Cross-Lagged Panel Models
text:
32.1 Two Variables, Evolving Together
Suppose you want to understand the relationship between anxiety and depression over time. Does anxiety precede and predict depression? Does depression feed back into anxiety? Or do they mutually reinforce each other?
To answer this, you need data on both variables at multiple time points, and you need a model that can disentangle the temporal ordering of their relationship. The cross-lagged panel model (CLPM) is the standard tool for this question.

title: 31  Cross-Lagged Panel Models
text:
32.2 The Classic CLPM
In a two-wave, two-variable CLPM with variables A (anxiety) and D (depression):
\[A_{t+1} = a_{AA} \cdot A_t + a_{DA} \cdot D_t + \varepsilon_{A,t+1}\] \[D_{t+1} = a_{DD} \cdot D_t + a_{AD} \cdot A_t + \varepsilon_{D,t+1}\]
Four parameters: - \(a_{AA}\), \(a_{DD}\): autoregressive effects — stability of each variable over time - \(a_{DA}\): cross-lagged effect of D at time \(t\) on A at time \(t+1\) - \(a_{AD}\): cross-lagged effect of A at time \(t\) on D at time \(t+1\)


Code
# Simulate 3-wave panel data
n <- 200
n_waves <- 3

# True DGP: anxiety -> depression (not the reverse)
A <- matrix(NA, n, n_waves)
D <- matrix(NA, n, n_waves)

A[, 1] <- rnorm(n, 50, 8)
D[, 1] <- rnorm(n, 45, 7)

for (t in 2:n_waves) {
  A[, t] <- 0.6 * A[, t-1] + 0.1 * D[, t-1] + rnorm(n, 0, 5)  # small D->A effect
  D[, t] <- 0.6 * D[, t-1] + 0.3 * A[, t-1] + rnorm(n, 0, 5)  # strong A->D effect
}

df_clpm <- as.data.frame(cbind(A, D)) |>
  setNames(c(paste0("A", 1:3), paste0("D", 1:3)))




Code
# Classic CLPM in lavaan
clpm_model <- '
  # Autoregressive effects
  A2 ~ a_AA * A1
  A3 ~ a_AA * A2
  D2 ~ a_DD * D1
  D3 ~ a_DD * D2

  # Cross-lagged effects
  A2 ~ a_DA * D1
  A3 ~ a_DA * D2
  D2 ~ a_AD * A1
  D3 ~ a_AD * A2

  # Residual covariances (within same wave)
  A2 ~~ D2
  A3 ~~ D3

  # Variance of wave 1
  A1 ~~ A1
  D1 ~~ D1
  A1 ~~ D1
'

m_clpm <- sem(clpm_model, data = df_clpm)
parameterEstimates(m_clpm) |>
  filter(op == "~") |>
  select(lhs, rhs, label, est, se, pvalue) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "CLPM: cross-lagged path from A→D is strong; D→A is weak")

title: 31  Cross-Lagged Panel Models
text:
32.3 The Problem with Classic CLPM
The classic CLPM conflates within-person dynamics with between-person differences. Consider: people who are chronically anxious tend to also be chronically depressed (stable between-person correlation). The cross-lagged paths in the classic CLPM pick up both this stable between-person covariation and the genuine within-person dynamic — they cannot be separated.
This means even if there is no within-person causal relationship between anxiety and depression (i.e., changes in anxiety within a person do not cause changes in depression), the classic CLPM can show significant “cross-lagged” effects simply because chronically anxious people are also chronically depressed.

title: 31  Cross-Lagged Panel Models
text:
32.4 The Random Intercept CLPM (RI-CLPM)
Hamaker, Kuiper, & Grasman (2015) proposed the Random Intercept CLPM, which solves this by separating two sources of variation:

Between-person: Stable trait levels (random intercepts) that explain why some people are chronically higher on A or D
Within-person: Fluctuations around each person’s stable level, and the cross-lagged dynamics among those fluctuations



Code
ri_clpm_model <- '
  # Random intercepts (stable between-person levels)
  RI_A =~ 1*A1 + 1*A2 + 1*A3
  RI_D =~ 1*D1 + 1*D2 + 1*D3

  # Within-person components (deviations from stable level)
  wA1 =~ 1*A1; wA2 =~ 1*A2; wA3 =~ 1*A3
  wD1 =~ 1*D1; wD2 =~ 1*D2; wD3 =~ 1*D3

  # Constrain observed variances to 0 (all captured by RI + within)
  A1 ~~ 0*A1; A2 ~~ 0*A2; A3 ~~ 0*A3
  D1 ~~ 0*D1; D2 ~~ 0*D2; D3 ~~ 0*D3

  # Within-person cross-lagged dynamics
  wA2 ~ a_AA*wA1 + a_DA*wD1
  wA3 ~ a_AA*wA2 + a_DA*wD2
  wD2 ~ a_DD*wD1 + a_AD*wA1
  wD3 ~ a_DD*wD2 + a_AD*wA2

  # Random intercept variances and covariance
  RI_A ~~ RI_A; RI_D ~~ RI_D; RI_A ~~ RI_D

  # Within-person residual variances
  wA1 ~~ wA1; wD1 ~~ wD1
  wA2 ~~ wA2; wD2 ~~ wD2
  wA3 ~~ wA3; wD3 ~~ wD3
  wA2 ~~ wD2; wA3 ~~ wD3

  # Mean structure
  RI_A ~ 1; RI_D ~ 1
  wA1 ~ 0; wD1 ~ 0
'

m_ri_clpm <- sem(ri_clpm_model, data = df_clpm, estimator = "ML")

parameterEstimates(m_ri_clpm) |>
  filter(op == "~", lhs %in% c("wA2","wA3","wD2","wD3")) |>

32.5 Comparing CLPM vs. RI-CLPM

WarningAssumption: Stationarity of Cross-Lagged Effects



What it means: The CLPM (and RI-CLPM) typically constrains cross-lagged effects to be equal across waves. This assumes the dynamics do not change over the study period.
How to check: Fit an unconstrained model (different path coefficients at each wave) and compare fit to the constrained model.








WarningAssumption: No Omitted Dynamics



What it means: Both models assume all relevant variables are included. An omitted third variable that drives both A and D could produce spurious cross-lagged effects.
How to check: Think carefully about what else changes over time that could explain both variables. Include plausible third variables as additional nodes.

title: 32  Overview
text: Module 5: Experimental Designs with Nesting

title: 32  Overview
text:
32.1 Experiments Are Not Flat
A well-designed randomized experiment is often treated as the gold standard for causal inference. And it is — when properly analyzed. The problem is that most experiments are analyzed as if every observation is an independent draw from a single population. They are not.
Even the simplest between-subjects experiment has nesting baked in:

Participants are a sample from a population of possible participants
Stimuli (vignettes, words, products, images) are a sample from a population of possible stimuli
Conditions may be nested within experimenters, sessions, labs, or time periods

When stimuli are treated as fixed — as if your 20 vignettes are the only 20 that exist — the generalization to other stimuli is silent. Your p-values are too small because stimulus-level variance is being absorbed into the residual, artificially tightening standard errors.
Judd, Westfall, & Kenny (2012, 2017) documented that this mistake is pervasive in social psychology and leads to systematic Type I error inflation.

title: 32  Overview
text:
32.2 What This Module Covers
Part 1: Crossed Random Effects in Experiments — Why stimuli are random, what happens when you treat them as fixed, and how to fix it with crossed random effects models in lme4.
Part 2: Partially Nested Designs — When one condition has nesting (e.g., treatment participants have therapists/coaches) and the other does not, standard MLM misspecifies the design. The Bauer et al. partially nested model handles this correctly.
Part 3: Maximal vs. Parsimonious Random Effects — The Barr et al. (2013) vs. Matuschek et al. (2017) debate: how complex should your random effects structure be? When does complexity help and when does it hurt?
Part 4: Multi-Site Experiments — When the same experiment runs at multiple sites (labs, countries, platforms), sites introduce clustering. The many-labs design and how to model treatment effect heterogeneity across sites.
Part 5: Power Analysis for Nested Designs — Why standard power formulas are wrong for nested data, the role of ICC, simulation-based power analysis, and practical sample size guidance.

title: 33  Crossed Random Effects in Experiments
text: 34 Crossed Random Effects in Experiments
crumbs: 33  Crossed Random Effects in Experiments

title: 33  Crossed Random Effects in Experiments
text:
34.1 The Stimuli Problem
Imagine a social psychology experiment studying whether a message framed as a gain (“you will save $100”) versus a loss (“you will lose $100”) influences willingness to act. You write 12 gain-framed messages and 12 loss-framed messages. Each of 80 participants reads 6 messages (3 gain, 3 loss) and rates their likelihood of acting.
The standard analysis: participant is the random factor, message condition is the fixed factor. This treats your 12+12 messages as if they were the only gain and loss messages that exist — as if your results generalize to the condition (framing), not to the population of possible messages.
But your 12 gain messages are a sample from all possible gain-framing messages. Some are compelling; others are weak. If you happened to write unusually persuasive loss messages, your “framing effect” is inflated. The variance among messages is not residual noise — it is a second source of random variation that your model must account for.
Failing to include message as a random factor inflates Type I error. Judd, Westfall & Kenny (2012) showed this using the “languages as fixed effects fallacy” as the model, but the problem extends to any stimulus set.
crumbs: 33  Crossed Random Effects in Experiments

title: 33  Crossed Random Effects in Experiments
text:
34.2 The Crossed Random Effects Solution
Both participants and messages are random. Every participant sees multiple messages; every message is seen by multiple participants. They are crossed — neither is nested within the other.


Code
n_participants <- 60
n_messages     <- 24  # 12 gain, 12 loss

participant_effects <- rnorm(n_participants, 0, 8)
message_effects     <- rnorm(n_messages, 0, 6)
message_condition   <- rep(c("gain", "loss"), each = 12)

# Each participant sees 6 messages (3 gain, 3 loss)
# We'll simulate a fully crossed subset for clarity
df_exp <- expand_grid(
  participant = 1:n_participants,
  message     = 1:n_messages
) |>
  filter((message <= 12 & participant %% 2 == 1) |
         (message > 12  & participant %% 2 == 0) |
         sample(c(TRUE, FALSE), n(), replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.25, 0.75))) |>
  mutate(
    condition    = message_condition[message],
    p_eff        = participant_effects[participant],
    m_eff        = message_effects[message],
    # True framing effect: loss > gain by 3 points
    WTP          = 50 + 3 * (condition == "loss") + p_eff + m_eff + rnorm(n(), 0, 5),
    participant  = factor(participant),
    message      = factor(message)
  )




Code
# Wrong: only participant as random effect (ignores message variance)
m_wrong <- lmer(WTP ~ condition + (1 | participant), data = df_exp)

# Correct: both participant and message as random effects
m_correct <- lmer(WTP ~ condition + (1 | participant) + (1 | message), data = df_exp)

# Compare SE for the condition effect
bind_rows(
  broom.mixed::tidy(m_wrong,   effects = "fixed") |> filter(term == "conditionloss") |>
    mutate(model = "Wrong (no message RE)"),
  broom.mixed::tidy(m_correct, effects = "fixed") |> filter(term == "conditionloss") |>
    mutate(model = "Correct (participant + message RE)")
) |>
  select(model, estimate, std.error, statistic, p.value) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "Ignoring message variance shrinks the SE — anti-conservative inference")



Ignoring message variance shrinks the SE — anti-conservative inference

title: 33  Crossed Random Effects in Experiments
text:
34.3 Assumptions






WarningAssumption: Both Participants and Stimuli are Representative Samples



What it means: The crossed random effects model generalizes over both the sampled participants and the sampled stimuli. If your stimuli are not representative of the population you want to generalize to (e.g., you hand-selected the most extreme examples), the generalization is invalid.
How to check: Reflect on how your stimuli were selected. Were they sampled systematically, or chosen to maximize the effect? If the latter, your results may not replicate with a different stimulus set.








WarningAssumption: Random Effects are Independent of the Experimental Manipulation



What it means: The random intercept for message \(m\) should be unrelated to whether that message is in the gain or loss condition. This is automatically satisfied in a well-designed experiment where condition is randomly assigned to messages.
How to check: In a fully crossed design (every participant sees every message), this is guaranteed. In partial crossing, verify that assignment was randomized.
crumbs: 33  Crossed Random Effects in Experiments

title: 33  Crossed Random Effects in Experiments
section: 34.4 The Westfall, Kenny & Judd (2014) Power Argument
text:
34.4 The Westfall, Kenny & Judd (2014) Power Argument
Westfall, Kenny & Judd (2014) extended this analysis to show that power depends on both the number of participants and the number of stimuli. Adding more participants when you have only a few stimuli can give you very little additional power — because stimulus-level variance is the binding constraint.
This has direct implications for sample size planning: you should increase your stimulus set, not just your participant count.
crumbs: 33  Crossed Random Effects in Experiments

title: 34  Partially Nested Designs
text: 35 Partially Nested Designs

title: 34  Partially Nested Designs
section: 35.1 When Only One Condition Has Clustering
text:
35.1 When Only One Condition Has Clustering
Consider a psychotherapy trial. The treatment group receives group therapy: 8 clients per therapist, 15 therapists. The control group receives no treatment (waitlist) — there is no therapist and no group structure. Clients in the control condition are independent of each other.
This design is partially nested: the treatment condition has a natural cluster structure (clients within therapists/groups), but the control condition does not.
If you fit a standard two-level model treating all participants the same way, you either: 1. Ignore clustering in the treatment group — deflating standard errors and inflating Type I error for the treatment effect 2. Apply random effects to all participants — misspecifying the control group, which has no actual clustering
The partially nested model (Bauer, Sterba & Hallfors, 2008) handles this correctly by allowing different variance structures in different conditions.

title: 34  Partially Nested Designs
text:
35.2 Simulating a Partially Nested Design


Code
n_therapists <- 15
n_per_group  <- 8
n_control    <- n_therapists * n_per_group  # same total N

# Treatment group: therapist clustering (ICC ≈ 0.15)
therapist_effects <- rnorm(n_therapists, 0, 3)

df_treatment <- tibble(
  condition   = "treatment",
  therapist   = rep(1:n_therapists, each = n_per_group),
  client      = 1:(n_therapists * n_per_group),
  therapist_e = rep(therapist_effects, each = n_per_group),
  outcome     = 50 + 5 + therapist_e + rnorm(n_therapists * n_per_group, 0, 6)
)

# Control group: no clustering (independent)
df_control <- tibble(
  condition   = "control",
  therapist   = NA_integer_,  # no therapist
  client      = 1:n_control + max(df_treatment$client),
  therapist_e = 0,
  outcome     = 50 + rnorm(n_control, 0, 6)
)

df_pn <- bind_rows(df_treatment, df_control)

title: 34  Partially Nested Designs
section: 35.3 The Partially Nested Model in lme4
text:
35.3 The Partially Nested Model in lme4
The trick: create a dummy therapist variable for control participants (unique ID for each, indicating no shared clustering) and fit a model where the random effect only applies to the treatment group.


Code
# Assign unique "therapist" IDs to control participants
df_pn <- df_pn |>
  mutate(
    cluster_id = ifelse(condition == "treatment",
                        paste0("T", therapist),
                        paste0("C", client))  # each control is own "cluster"
  )

# Partially nested: cluster RE only in treatment
# Use dummy coding and interaction for the random effect
df_pn <- df_pn |>
  mutate(treat_dummy = as.integer(condition == "treatment"))

# Fit: allow random effect only within treatment clusters
m_pn <- lmer(outcome ~ condition + (1 | cluster_id), data = df_pn)

# Compare to naive model (ignores all clustering)
m_naive <- lm(outcome ~ condition, data = df_pn)

bind_rows(
  broom::tidy(m_naive) |> filter(term == "conditiontreatment") |>
    mutate(model = "Naive (no clustering)"),
  broom.mixed::tidy(m_pn, effects = "fixed") |>
    filter(term == "conditiontreatment") |>
    mutate(model = "Partially nested model")
) |>
  select(model, estimate, std.error, p.value) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "Partially nested model: correct SE for clustered treatment group")

WarningAssumption: Correct Identification of Which Condition is Clustered



What it means: You must correctly specify which condition has clustering and which does not. If clustering is misattributed (e.g., you apply cluster random effects to the control group when there is none), variance components will be estimated incorrectly.
How to check: Draw the design. Which participants share a natural group, coach, therapist, or classroom? That condition has the nested structure.








WarningAssumption: Homogeneity of Within-Cluster Variance



What it means: The standard partially nested model assumes the residual variance is the same in the treatment and control groups (only the random effect variance differs).
How to check: Compare within-group variance across conditions. If the treatment group has substantially more or less residual variance than control, allow heteroskedastic residuals.

title: 35  Maximal vs. Parsimonious Random Effects
text: 36 Maximal vs. Parsimonious Random Effects
crumbs: 35  Maximal vs. Parsimonious Random Effects

title: 35  Maximal vs. Parsimonious Random Effects
text:
36.1 The Debate
In 2013, Barr, Levy, Scheepers, & Tily published an influential paper titled “Random Effects Structure for Confirmatory Hypothesis Testing: Keep It Maximal.” Their argument:

To control Type I error at the nominal level, researchers should include the maximal random effects structure — every random slope that is justified by the experimental design.

In a within-subjects design where every participant sees every condition, you must include a random slope for condition by participants. Omitting this random slope will produce anti-conservative p-values.
Two years later, Matuschek, Kliegl, Vasishth, Baayen, & Bates (2017) pushed back in a paper titled “Balancing Type I Error and Power in Linear Mixed Models.” Their argument:

Maximal models frequently fail to converge or produce degenerate variance components. Over-parameterized models can actually have worse power than well-specified parsimonious models. Use likelihood ratio tests to decide what to include.

Both papers are correct. The question is when each applies.
crumbs: 35  Maximal vs. Parsimonious Random Effects

title: 35  Maximal vs. Parsimonious Random Effects
text:
36.2 Why Maximal Models Can Fail


Code
# Simulate a within-subjects design
# Each participant sees each condition 4 times (trials)
# 40 participants × 3 conditions × 4 trials = 480 observations
# Random effects needed: 40 × 3 = 120 — well below 480
n_participants <- 40
n_conditions   <- 3
n_trials       <- 4

participant_intercepts <- rnorm(n_participants, 50, 8)
# Small (but nonzero) random slopes
participant_slopes     <- rnorm(n_participants, 0, 0.5)

df_within <- expand_grid(
  participant = 1:n_participants,
  condition   = 1:n_conditions,
  trial       = 1:n_trials
) |>
  mutate(
    condition_eff = c(-2, 0, 2)[condition],  # condition effect
    p_slope       = participant_slopes[participant],
    Y = participant_intercepts[participant] + condition_eff +
        p_slope * condition_eff + rnorm(n(), 0, 5),
    participant = factor(participant),
    condition   = factor(condition)
  )

# Maximal model
m_max <- lmer(Y ~ condition + (condition | participant),
              data    = df_within,
              control = lmerControl(optimizer = "bobyqa"))

# Does it converge?
library(performance)
check_convergence(m_max)

attr(,"gradient")

# Parsimonious model (random slope variance is tiny — can drop it)
m_pars <- lmer(Y ~ condition + (1 | participant), data = df_within)

title: 35  Maximal vs. Parsimonious Random Effects
text:
36.3 The Decision Framework
The current consensus among methodologists:






NoteWhen to Use Maximal Random Effects




Within-subjects experimental manipulations: Always include random slopes for within-subjects factors. This is the critical case. Barr et al. (2013) are correct: omitting random slopes for within-subject conditions inflates Type I error.
When theory or pilot data suggests heterogeneous effects: If you have prior reason to believe the treatment effect varies substantially across participants, include the random slope.
Large samples: With enough data, complex models converge and the efficiency loss from over-parameterization is small.









NoteWhen to Simplify (Parsimonious)




Convergence failures: If the maximal model does not converge, you must simplify. Start by removing the correlation between random intercept and slope ((1 | participant) + (0 + condition | participant)). Then consider removing the random slope itself.
Degenerate variance components: If the random slope variance is estimated at exactly zero (or very near), it is contributing nothing and can be dropped.
Very small variance components: Use an LRT to test whether the random slope significantly improves fit. If not, the parsimonious model is appropriate.
crumbs: 35  Maximal vs. Parsimonious Random Effects

title: 35  Maximal vs. Parsimonious Random Effects
text:
36.4 A Practical Simplification Protocol


Code
# Step 1: Try the maximal model
m_max_2 <- tryCatch(
  lmer(Y ~ condition + (condition | participant),
       data = df_within, control = lmerControl(optimizer = "bobyqa")),
  warning = function(w) { cat("Warning:", conditionMessage(w), "\n"); NULL }
)

# Step 2: If it fails/has issues, remove correlation first
m_no_corr <- lmer(Y ~ condition + (1 | participant) + (0 + condition | participant),
                  data = df_within)

# Step 3: Test: does the random slope improve fit?
anova(m_pars, m_no_corr)

WarningAssumption: The Random Effects Structure Matches the Design



What it means: The random effects you include should reflect the actual nesting structure of your design. A random slope for a between-subjects factor makes no statistical sense (between-subjects factors cannot vary within a subject).
Common mistake: Including a random slope for a between-subjects condition. This will either produce a degenerate model or absorb all variance in a nonsensical way.
How to check: For each predictor, ask: does this variable vary within the grouping unit (participant, stimulus)? If yes, it can have a random slope for that unit. If no (between-subjects), it cannot.

title: 36  Multi-Site Experiments
text: 37 Multi-Site Experiments

title: 36  Multi-Site Experiments
text:
37.1 Generalizability is a Nesting Problem
The replication crisis in psychology revealed that many findings were fragile — they held at one university, with one sample, run by one experimenter, but failed to replicate elsewhere. The problem is that site is a nesting variable that standard analysis ignores.
A multi-site experiment (also called a many-labs design) runs the same study across multiple locations: different universities, countries, online platforms, or time periods. Each site contributes data. Sites are treated as a random sample from a larger population of sites.
This design answers a richer question: not “does this effect occur at location X?” but “does this effect occur, on average, across the population of contexts?” And it allows you to ask: how much does the effect size vary across sites?

title: 36  Multi-Site Experiments
text:
37.2 The Model
Sites are random. The treatment effect may vary across sites (cross-level interaction between treatment and site).
\[Y_{ijk} = \gamma_{00} + \gamma_{10} \text{Treat}_{ij} + u_{0k} + u_{1k} \text{Treat}_{ij} + \varepsilon_{ijk}\]
Where: - \(k\) = site; \(j\) = participant; \(i\) = observation - \(u_{0k}\): site-level random intercept (baseline differences across sites) - \(u_{1k}\): site-level random slope for treatment (heterogeneity in treatment effect) - \(\gamma_{10}\): average treatment effect across sites


Code
n_sites       <- 20
n_per_site    <- 40  # 20 treatment, 20 control

site_intercepts <- rnorm(n_sites, 50, 5)   # sites differ in baseline
site_effects    <- rnorm(n_sites, 5, 3)    # treatment effect varies across sites
# Average effect = 5; some sites show 8, some show 2

df_ml <- expand_grid(
  site = 1:n_sites,
  participant = 1:n_per_site
) |>
  mutate(
    treatment   = rep(c(rep(0, 20), rep(1, 20)), n_sites),
    site_base   = site_intercepts[site],
    site_effect = site_effects[site],
    outcome     = site_base + site_effect * treatment + rnorm(n(), 0, 6),
    site        = factor(site)
  )




Code
# Random intercepts and slopes for sites
m_ml <- lmer(outcome ~ treatment + (treatment | site), data = df_ml)

broom.mixed::tidy(m_ml, effects = "fixed") |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "Average treatment effect across sites")

title: 36  Multi-Site Experiments
text:
37.3 The ICC for Treatment Effects
The random slope for treatment tells you how much the effect varies across sites. The I² statistic (from meta-analysis) provides a related measure: what proportion of the total variation in effect size estimates is due to genuine heterogeneity vs. sampling error?


Code
# Variance of the treatment effect across sites
var_treatment_effect <- as.data.frame(VarCorr(m_ml))$vcov[2]  # slope variance
cat("Standard deviation of treatment effect across sites:",
    round(sqrt(var_treatment_effect), 2), "\n")


Standard deviation of treatment effect across sites: 2.7 


Code
cat("Average effect:", round(fixef(m_ml)["treatment"], 2), "\n")


Average effect: 5.04 


Code
cat("Effect ranges approximately from",
    round(fixef(m_ml)["treatment"] - 2*sqrt(var_treatment_effect), 1),
    "to",
    round(fixef(m_ml)["treatment"] + 2*sqrt(var_treatment_effect), 1),
    "across sites\n")


Effect ranges approximately from -0.4 to 10.4 across sites








WarningAssumption: Sites are a Representative Sample



What it means: The multi-site model generalizes to the population of sites from which your sites were drawn. If your 20 labs are all top-10 research universities, the results generalize to that population — not to community colleges, online platforms, or international contexts.
How to check: Describe your site-selection process. Were sites systematically sampled? Opportunistically recruited? The inference should match the selection.








WarningAssumption: Treatment Procedure is Comparable Across Sites



What it means: The “treatment” must be operationally equivalent across sites. If different labs implemented the procedure differently — different materials, different timing, different experimenter instructions — site-to-site variability reflects procedure differences, not true effect heterogeneity.
How to check: Pre-specify and enforce standardized protocols. Measure implementation fidelity. Include implementation fidelity as a moderator.

title: 37  Power Analysis for Nested Designs
text: 38 Power Analysis for Nested Designs
crumbs: 37  Power Analysis for Nested Designs

title: 37  Power Analysis for Nested Designs
section: 38.1 Why Standard Power Formulas Are Wrong
text:
38.1 Why Standard Power Formulas Are Wrong
Standard power formulas (Cohen’s power tables, G*Power) assume independent observations. They compute: given my expected effect size and desired power, how many participants do I need?
In nested data, this question is incomplete. You also need to know: - How many clusters? (classrooms, firms, sites, therapists) - How many observations per cluster? - What is the ICC? (how similar are observations within clusters)
The ICC and cluster size together determine the design effect (DEFF), which shrinks your effective sample size:
\[N_{effective} = \frac{N}{1 + (n_{cluster} - 1) \times \text{ICC}}\]
Ignoring this leads to severely underpowered studies.
crumbs: 37  Power Analysis for Nested Designs

title: 37  Power Analysis for Nested Designs
text:
38.2 The ICC-Power Relationship


Code
# Power as a function of ICC, for a fixed N = 300 in clusters of 15
compute_power <- function(icc, n_total = 300, cluster_size = 15, effect = 0.3, alpha = 0.05) {
  deff    <- 1 + (cluster_size - 1) * icc
  n_eff   <- n_total / deff
  # Two-sample t-test power with effective N
  power.t.test(n = n_eff / 2, delta = effect, sd = 1, sig.level = alpha)$power
}

icc_vals <- seq(0, 0.5, by = 0.01)
power_vals <- sapply(icc_vals, compute_power)

tibble(ICC = icc_vals, Power = power_vals) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = ICC, y = Power)) +
  geom_line(color = "#1e3a5f", linewidth = 1.3) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0.80, linetype = "dashed", color = "#e63946") +
  annotate("text", x = 0.35, y = 0.82, label = "80% power threshold",
           color = "#e63946", size = 3.5) +
  labs(
    title    = "Power declines as ICC increases (N = 300, cluster size = 15)",
    subtitle = "Even modest ICC substantially reduces effective power",
    x        = "ICC",
    y        = "Statistical Power"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)









At ICC = 0, you have the expected power for 300 independent observations. At ICC = 0.2, power drops below 80% — you would need more observations to compensate.
crumbs: 37  Power Analysis for Nested Designs

title: 37  Power Analysis for Nested Designs
text:
38.3 Simulation-Based Power Analysis
For complex nested designs, analytic formulas do not always exist. The gold standard is simulation-based power analysis: generate data from the assumed DGP, fit the model, check whether the effect is significant, repeat 1000 times.


Code
simulate_power <- function(n_clusters, n_per_cluster, icc,
                            true_effect = 0.3, n_sims = 500) {
  sigma_between <- sqrt(icc)
  sigma_within  <- sqrt(1 - icc)

  p_values <- replicate(n_sims, {
    # Simulate two groups of clusters
    n_c <- ceiling(n_clusters / 2)
    cluster_effects <- rnorm(n_clusters, 0, sigma_between)

    df <- tibble(
      cluster = rep(1:n_clusters, each = n_per_cluster),
      group   = ifelse(cluster <= n_c, 0, 1)
    ) |>
      mutate(
        Y = true_effect * group + cluster_effects[cluster] +
            rnorm(n(), 0, sigma_within)
      )

    tryCatch({
      m <- lmer(Y ~ group + (1 | cluster), data = df, REML = FALSE)
      coef(summary(m))["group", "Pr(>|t|)"]
    }, error = function(e) NA_real_,
       warning = function(w) {
         m <- lmer(Y ~ group + (1 | cluster), data = df, REML = FALSE)
         tryCatch(coef(summary(m))["group", "Pr(>|t|)"],
                  error = function(e) NA_real_)
       })
  })
  p_values <- p_values[!is.na(p_values)]

  mean(p_values < 0.05)
}

# Power across different designs
designs <- expand_grid(
  n_clusters      = c(10, 20, 30, 40),
  n_per_cluster   = c(10, 20),
  icc             = c(0.05, 0.15)
) |>
  mutate(
    power = pmap_dbl(
      list(n_clusters, n_per_cluster, icc),
      ~simulate_power(..1, ..2, ..3, n_sims = 200)  # reduce for speed
    ),
    total_N = n_clusters * n_per_cluster,
    design  = paste0("ICC=", icc, ", n/cluster=", n_per_cluster)
  )

ggplot(designs, aes(x = n_clusters, y = power, color = design, linetype = design)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) + geom_point(size = 2.5) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0.80, linetype = "dotted", color = "gray50") +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  labs(
    title    = "Power by number of clusters (simulated)",
    subtitle = "Effect size d = 0.3; n_sims = 200 per design",
    x        = "Number of Clusters",
    y        = "Power",
    color    = "Design", linetype = "Design"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)
crumbs: 37  Power Analysis for Nested Designs

title: 37  Power Analysis for Nested Designs
section: 38.4 The simr Package for Complex Designs
text:
38.4 The simr Package for Complex Designs
For multilevel models with random slopes, crossed random effects, or other complex structures, the simr package provides a convenient simulation-based power analysis directly from a fitted model:


Code
library(simr)

# Fit a model with your expected parameters
m_pilot <- lmer(Y ~ group + (1 | cluster),
                data = your_pilot_data)

# Change the effect size to your expected value
fixef(m_pilot)["group"] <- 0.3

# Simulate power for different sample sizes
power_curve <- powerCurve(m_pilot,
                           along = "cluster",   # vary number of clusters
                           nsim  = 200)
plot(power_curve)
crumbs: 37  Power Analysis for Nested Designs

title: 37  Power Analysis for Nested Designs
text:
38.5 Key Takeaways for Power Planning






NoteRules of Thumb




More clusters > more per cluster when ICC is high. If ICC = 0.20, doubling the number of clusters gains much more power than doubling the size of each cluster.
The ICC threshold: If ICC > 0.10 and cluster size > 10, standard power formulas underestimate required N by 50–100%.
For crossed designs (participants × stimuli): Power depends on both the number of participants and the number of stimuli. Westfall, Kenny & Judd (2014) showed that many published studies are severely underpowered on the stimulus side.
Collect pilot ICC estimates: Run a small pilot study and compute the ICC before committing to a full design.
crumbs: 37  Power Analysis for Nested Designs

title: 38  Overview
text: Module 6: Extended Toolkit

title: 38  Overview
text:
38.1 Beyond Linear Outcomes
The previous modules built the core framework for nested data analysis, assuming a continuous, approximately normally distributed outcome. Most of the real world does not cooperate with that assumption.
Outcomes in social science research are often: - Binary: Did the customer purchase? Did the patient recover? Did the participant choose option A? - Counts: How many times did the participant make an aggressive move? How many days was the patient hospitalized? - Time-to-event: When did the employee quit? When did the patient relapse? When did the firm go bankrupt?
And the data have additional complications: - Missing observations that are not randomly missing — the patients who dropped out are systematically different from those who stayed - Effect sizes that are hard to interpret because variance exists at multiple levels
This module addresses each of these extensions.

title: 38  Overview
text:
38.2 What This Module Covers
Part 1: Binary Outcomes — Logistic MLM — Generalized linear mixed models for binary outcomes. The crucial distinction between marginal and conditional effects, which matters enormously for multilevel logistic models.
Part 2: Count Outcomes — Poisson MLM — Count data, overdispersion, zero-inflation, and when to use negative binomial models.
Part 3: Survival Analysis — Time-to-event data, the hazard function, Kaplan-Meier curves, Cox proportional hazards models, and frailty models for clustered survival data.
Part 4: Missing Data in Nested Contexts — Missing data mechanisms, why longitudinal dropout is especially problematic, and multiple imputation strategies for nested data.
Part 5: Effect Sizes & Reporting — The challenges of R² in multilevel models, marginal vs. conditional R², ICC as an effect size, and how to write up and report multilevel analyses.

title: 39  Binary Outcomes (Logistic MLM)
text: 40 Binary Outcomes: Logistic Mixed Models

title: 39  Binary Outcomes (Logistic MLM)
text:
40.1 When Your Outcome is 0 or 1
Suppose you are studying whether a price promotion increases purchase probability, using transaction data from 5,000 customers across 50 stores. Your outcome is binary: purchased (1) or not (0). Customers are nested within stores.
A standard logistic regression ignores the store clustering. A logistic mixed model accounts for it — allowing stores to differ in their baseline purchase rates, and estimating the promotion effect while accounting for this.

title: 39  Binary Outcomes (Logistic MLM)
text:
40.2 The Model
The logistic mixed model extends the standard logistic regression by adding a random intercept (and optionally a random slope):
\[\text{logit}(P(Y_{ij} = 1)) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_{ij} + u_{0j}\]
Where \(u_{0j} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2_u)\) is the store-level random intercept.


Code
n_stores    <- 50
n_customers <- 100  # per store

store_effects <- rnorm(n_stores, 0, 1)  # store baseline differences

df_purchase <- expand_grid(
  store    = 1:n_stores,
  customer = 1:n_customers
) |>
  mutate(
    promotion   = rbinom(n(), 1, 0.5),
    store_eff   = store_effects[store],
    log_odds    = -1.5 + 0.8 * promotion + store_eff,
    purchased   = rbinom(n(), 1, plogis(log_odds)),
    store       = factor(store)
  )

# Logistic mixed model
m_logit <- glmer(purchased ~ promotion + (1 | store),
                 family  = binomial,
                 data    = df_purchase)

summary(m_logit)

title: 39  Binary Outcomes (Logistic MLM)
section: 40.3 The Marginal vs. Conditional Effects Problem
text:
40.3 The Marginal vs. Conditional Effects Problem
This is the single most important subtlety in logistic MLMs, and it is often missed.
Conditional effect: The effect of promotion for a specific store at its mean random effect. This is what the model directly estimates.
Marginal effect: The effect of promotion averaged over the distribution of store effects — the population-average effect. This is usually what you want to report.
In linear models, marginal and conditional effects are equal. In logistic models, they are not. The marginal effect is always attenuated relative to the conditional effect — the more the random effects vary, the larger the difference.


Code
# Conditional predictions
pred_conditional <- ggpredict(m_logit, terms = "promotion",
                               type = "random")   # conditional on random effects = 0

# Marginal predictions (averaged over store distribution)
pred_marginal <- ggpredict(m_logit, terms = "promotion",
                            type = "fixed")   # marginal (fixed effects only)

bind_rows(
  as.data.frame(pred_conditional) |> mutate(type = "Conditional (store RE = 0)"),
  as.data.frame(pred_marginal)    |> mutate(type = "Marginal (averaged over stores)")
) |>
  select(type, x, predicted, conf.low, conf.high) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "Conditional vs. marginal predicted purchase probability")

title: 39  Binary Outcomes (Logistic MLM)
text:
40.4 Assumptions






WarningAssumption 1: Correct Link Function



What it means: The logistic model assumes the log-odds of the outcome is linear in the predictors. This is the standard assumption for binary outcomes, but it may not hold at the extremes.
How to check: Examine whether predicted probabilities are reasonable at extreme predictor values. Consider complementary log-log (cloglog) as an alternative if your outcome is rare (P < 0.05).








WarningAssumption 2: Normality of Random Effects (on the Log-Odds Scale)



What it means: The store-level random intercepts are assumed normal on the log-odds scale. If store effects are heavily skewed (e.g., a few stores have dramatically different purchase rates), this assumption may fail.
How to check: Extract random effects with ranef() and examine their distribution. A Q-Q plot of the random intercepts is informative.


Code
re_vals <- ranef(m_logit)$store[, 1]
qqnorm(re_vals, main = "Q-Q plot of store random effects")
qqline(re_vals, col = "#e63946", lwd = 2)

















WarningAssumption 3: No Overdispersion



What it means: For binary outcomes, overdispersion (more variance than the model predicts) can occur if there is residual clustering not accounted for by the random effects.
How to check: Compute the ratio of the Pearson chi-square statistic to its degrees of freedom. Values substantially greater than 1 indicate overdispersion.

title: 40  Count Outcomes (Poisson MLM)
text: 41 Count Outcomes: Poisson and Negative Binomial MLMs

title: 40  Count Outcomes (Poisson MLM)
section: 41.1 When Your Outcome is a Count
text:
41.1 When Your Outcome is a Count
Count outcomes arise frequently in psychology and social science: the number of aggressive acts in a session, the number of days absent from work, the number of times a consumer repurchases, the number of words recalled.
Counts have two properties that make standard linear regression inappropriate: 1. They are non-negative integers (you cannot have −3 absences) 2. Their variance often increases with their mean (heteroskedasticity is built-in)
The Poisson distribution is the natural model for counts. The Poisson mixed model extends it to nested data.

title: 40  Count Outcomes (Poisson MLM)
text:
41.2 The Poisson Mixed Model
\[\log(\mu_{ij}) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_{ij} + u_{0j}, \quad Y_{ij} \sim \text{Poisson}(\mu_{ij})\]
The log link ensures predictions are always positive. Coefficients are interpreted as log rate ratios, or more intuitively as rate ratios after exponentiation.


Code
n_participants <- 60
n_days         <- 14

participant_effects <- rnorm(n_participants, 0, 0.5)

df_count <- expand_grid(person = 1:n_participants, day = 1:n_days) |>
  mutate(
    treatment = rep(rbinom(n_participants, 1, 0.5), each = n_days),
    p_eff     = rep(participant_effects, each = n_days),
    log_mu    = 1.5 - 0.5 * treatment + p_eff,  # true rate ratio = exp(-0.5) ≈ 0.61
    count     = rpois(n(), lambda = exp(log_mu)),
    person    = factor(person)
  )

m_pois <- glmer(count ~ treatment + (1 | person),
                family = poisson,
                data   = df_count)

broom.mixed::tidy(m_pois, effects = "fixed", exponentiate = TRUE) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "Rate ratios: treatment reduces count outcome to 61% of control")

title: 40  Count Outcomes (Poisson MLM)
section: 41.3 Overdispersion: When Poisson is Not Enough
text:
41.3 Overdispersion: When Poisson is Not Enough
The Poisson distribution has a strict constraint: mean = variance. Real count data almost always has variance exceeding the mean — overdispersion. Sources of overdispersion include unobserved heterogeneity, clustering, and zero-inflation.


Code
# Check for overdispersion
pearson_residuals <- residuals(m_pois, type = "pearson")
dispersion_stat   <- sum(pearson_residuals^2) / df.residual(m_pois)
cat("Dispersion statistic:", round(dispersion_stat, 3),
    "(should be ~1 for Poisson; > 1.5 indicates overdispersion)\n")


Dispersion statistic: 1.022 (should be ~1 for Poisson; > 1.5 indicates overdispersion)


When overdispersion is present, use the negative binomial model, which adds a free dispersion parameter:


Code
# Simulate overdispersed count data
df_count_od <- df_count |>
  mutate(count_od = rnbinom(n(), mu = exp(log_mu), size = 2))  # overdispersed

m_nb <- glmmTMB(count_od ~ treatment + (1 | person),
                family = nbinom2,
                data   = df_count_od)

broom.mixed::tidy(m_nb, effects = "fixed", exponentiate = TRUE) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "Negative binomial rate ratios (handles overdispersion)")

title: 40  Count Outcomes (Poisson MLM)
text:
41.4 Zero-Inflation
Some count outcomes have more zeros than any Poisson or negative binomial model can account for. This is common when zeros arise from two different processes: (1) the structural zero — a participant who never engages in the behavior regardless of conditions, and (2) the sampling zero — a participant who could engage but happened not to on this occasion.


Code
# Zero-inflated count
df_count_zi <- df_count |>
  mutate(
    # Some people are "structural zeros" (never engage)
    structural_zero = rbinom(n(), 1, 0.3),
    count_zi = ifelse(structural_zero == 1, 0, count)
  )

cat("Proportion of zeros (standard Poisson):", mean(df_count$count == 0), "\n")


Proportion of zeros (standard Poisson): 0.1119048 


Code
cat("Proportion of zeros (zero-inflated):", mean(df_count_zi$count_zi == 0), "\n")


Proportion of zeros (zero-inflated): 0.3630952 


Code
m_zi <- glmmTMB(count_zi ~ treatment + (1 | person),
                ziformula = ~1,   # intercept-only model for zero-inflation
                family    = poisson,
                data      = df_count_zi)

broom.mixed::tidy(m_zi, effects = "fixed", component = "cond", exponentiate = TRUE) |>

title: 41  Survival Analysis
text: 42 Survival Analysis

title: 41  Survival Analysis
text:
42.1 When Time-to-Event is the Outcome
Not all outcomes are measured on a continuous scale or as a snapshot at one moment. Some outcomes are events: the moment an employee quits, the day a patient relapses, the first purchase after acquiring a new customer. The outcome of interest is when the event occurs — or whether it has occurred at all by the time observation ends.
This is the domain of survival analysis (also called event history analysis or time-to-event analysis).
The defining feature: censoring. Some participants have not experienced the event by the end of the study — they were still employed, still in remission, still active users when the observation window closed. Censored observations contribute partial information: we know the event had not occurred up to that point, but we do not know when (or whether) it eventually will.
Standard regression discards this information. Survival analysis uses it.

title: 41  Survival Analysis
text:
42.2 The Hazard Function
The central concept is the hazard function \(h(t)\): the instantaneous rate at which events occur at time \(t\), given that the event has not yet occurred by \(t\).
\[h(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t \le T < t + \Delta t \mid T \ge t)}{\Delta t}\]
Intuitively: if you have survived until time \(t\), what is your risk of the event occurring in the next small interval?

title: 41  Survival Analysis
text:
42.3 Kaplan-Meier Curves
The Kaplan-Meier estimator non-parametrically estimates the survival function \(S(t) = P(T > t)\): the probability of surviving (not experiencing the event) past time \(t\).


Code
# Simulate employee tenure data
n <- 200
group <- rep(c("High autonomy", "Low autonomy"), each = 100)

# High-autonomy employees stay longer
tenure_time  <- c(rexp(100, rate = 0.05),   # ~20 months average
                   rexp(100, rate = 0.10))   # ~10 months average
censored     <- rbinom(200, 1, 0.3)  # 30% are censored (still employed)
tenure_obs   <- pmin(tenure_time, 24)  # cap at 24 months observation window
status       <- ifelse(censored == 1, 0, 1)   # 0 = censored, 1 = event (quit)

df_surv <- tibble(time = tenure_obs, status, group)

# Kaplan-Meier
km_fit <- survfit(Surv(time, status) ~ group, data = df_surv)

ggsurvplot(
  km_fit,
  data        = df_surv,
  conf.int    = TRUE,
  pval        = TRUE,
  risk.table  = TRUE,
  palette     = c("#1e3a5f", "#aec6e8"),
  legend.labs = c("High autonomy", "Low autonomy"),
  xlab        = "Months",
  ylab        = "Probability of staying",
  title       = "Kaplan-Meier: employee tenure by autonomy condition"
)

title: 41  Survival Analysis
text:
42.4 The Cox Proportional Hazards Model
The Cox model estimates the effect of predictors on the hazard rate without specifying the baseline hazard function — a semiparametric approach:
\[h(t \mid X) = h_0(t) \cdot \exp(\beta_1 X_1 + \beta_2 X_2 + \ldots)\]
Coefficients are interpreted as hazard ratios after exponentiation: \(\exp(\beta) > 1\) means higher hazard (shorter survival); \(\exp(\beta) < 1\) means lower hazard (longer survival).


Code
# Add predictors
df_surv <- df_surv |>
  mutate(
    autonomy     = ifelse(group == "High autonomy", 1, 0),
    age          = rnorm(200, 35, 8),
    satisfaction = rnorm(200, 60, 10)
  )

m_cox <- coxph(Surv(time, status) ~ autonomy + age + satisfaction,
               data = df_surv)

broom::tidy(m_cox, exponentiate = TRUE) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "Cox hazard ratios: autonomy reduces hazard of quitting")

WarningAssumption: Proportional Hazards



What it means: The hazard ratio between groups is constant over time. The effect of autonomy on the hazard of quitting is the same in month 1 as in month 20.
Why it fails: Some effects are strongest early (a drug that prevents relapse mainly in the first few weeks) and fade over time. This is a non-proportional hazard.
How to check: Schoenfeld residuals should be uncorrelated with time. The cox.zph() function tests this.

ph_test <- cox.zph(m_cox)
print(ph_test)

title: 41  Survival Analysis
section: 42.5 Frailty Models for Clustered Survival Data
text:
42.5 Frailty Models for Clustered Survival Data
When survival times are clustered (patients within hospitals, employees within firms), standard Cox models underestimate standard errors. The frailty model adds a random effect (the “frailty”) to the Cox model — a direct analog of the random intercept in linear MLM.


Code
# Simulate clustered survival data
n_firms    <- 20
n_per_firm <- 10

firm_effects <- rnorm(n_firms, 0, 0.5)

df_cluster <- tibble(
  firm     = rep(1:n_firms, each = n_per_firm),
  autonomy = rbinom(n_firms * n_per_firm, 1, 0.5),
  firm_eff = rep(firm_effects, each = n_per_firm),
  time_raw = rexp(n_firms * n_per_firm,
                   rate = exp(-1.5 - 0.4 * autonomy + firm_eff)),
  status   = rbinom(n_firms * n_per_firm, 1, 0.7),
  time     = pmin(time_raw, 24)
)

# Cox model with gamma frailty
m_frailty <- coxph(Surv(time, status) ~ autonomy + frailty(firm),
                   data = df_cluster)

broom::tidy(m_frailty) |>
  filter(term == "autonomy") |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "Frailty model: firm clustering accounted for in survival analysis")

WarningAssumption: Non-Informative Censoring



What it means: The reason an observation is censored must be unrelated to the outcome. If sicker patients are more likely to drop out of a study (i.e., censoring is related to the hazard of relapse), standard survival estimates are biased.
How to check: Compare characteristics of censored and non-censored participants at their censoring time. Large differences suggest informative censoring.

title: 42  Missing Data in Nested Contexts
text: 43 Missing Data in Nested Contexts
crumbs: 42  Missing Data in Nested Contexts

title: 42  Missing Data in Nested Contexts
section: 43.1 Why Missing Data is a Nesting Problem
text:
43.1 Why Missing Data is a Nesting Problem
In a cross-sectional study, missing data is annoying but manageable: you lose some participants and hope they are not systematically different from those who stayed. In a longitudinal or nested study, missing data is woven into the very structure of the data — and it is almost always non-random.
Consider a clinical trial tracking depression over 12 months. Patients who are getting worse are more likely to drop out — they are too depressed to fill out questionnaires, or they seek alternative treatment. The patients who complete all 12 measurements are not a random sample. They are the patients who recovered (or at least stayed stable enough to participate). Any analysis based only on completers overestimates the treatment’s effectiveness.
This is attrition bias — but now understood through the lens of missing data mechanisms.
crumbs: 42  Missing Data in Nested Contexts

title: 42  Missing Data in Nested Contexts
text:
43.2 Missing Data Mechanisms
Three mechanisms, with very different implications:
MCAR (Missing Completely At Random): The probability of a value being missing is unrelated to any observed or unobserved variable. Your survey crashed and lost data for participants whose IDs ended in 7. Truly random. Analysis of complete cases is valid, though inefficient.
MAR (Missing At Random): The probability of missingness depends on observed variables, but not on the unobserved value itself. Older participants are more likely to miss the follow-up, but among older participants, the missing and observed values are similar. MAR allows valid inference with appropriate methods (mixed-effects models, multiple imputation).
MNAR (Missing Not At Random): The probability of missingness depends on the unobserved value. The most depressed patients are most likely to drop out — and depression is the outcome being measured. No standard method can fully correct for MNAR; sensitivity analysis is required.
crumbs: 42  Missing Data in Nested Contexts

title: 42  Missing Data in Nested Contexts
text:
43.3 Testing for Non-Random Dropout


Code
n_people <- 150
n_waves  <- 4

# Simulate longitudinal data with selective dropout
df_long <- expand_grid(person = 1:n_people, wave = 1:n_waves) |>
  group_by(person) |>
  mutate(
    baseline  = rnorm(1, 50, 10),
    treatment = rbinom(1, 1, 0.5),
    Y         = baseline - 3 * treatment * (wave - 1) / 3 + rnorm(n(), 0, 4),
    # Dropout: high-depression (high Y) people are more likely to dropout in wave 3+
    drop_prob = plogis(-3 + 0.04 * mean(Y[wave == 1])),
    dropout   = cummax(rbinom(n(), 1, drop_prob)),
    Y_obs     = ifelse(dropout == 1 & wave > 2, NA, Y)
  ) |>
  ungroup()

# Test: do completers differ from dropouts at baseline?
baseline_comparison <- df_long |>
  filter(wave == 1) |>
  mutate(completer = person %in% (df_long |>
    filter(wave == n_waves, !is.na(Y_obs)) |> pull(person))) |>
  group_by(completer) |>
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    mean_baseline = mean(baseline),
    mean_Y_wave1  = mean(Y)
  )

knitr::kable(baseline_comparison, digits = 2,
  caption = "Completers vs. dropouts at baseline: systematic differences indicate MAR/MNAR")

title: 42  Missing Data in Nested Contexts
text:
43.4 Mixed-Effects Models Under MAR
A key advantage of mixed-effects models: under the MAR assumption, the likelihood uses all available observations — not just complete cases. A patient who provided data at waves 1, 2, and 3 (but not 4) contributes to the model estimate for waves 1–3. You do not need to discard them.
This is why mixed-effects models are preferable to RM-ANOVA for longitudinal data with dropout: RM-ANOVA requires complete cases; mixed models do not.


Code
# Complete case analysis — keep only people with all 4 waves observed
df_complete <- df_long |>
  group_by(person) |>
  filter(sum(!is.na(Y_obs)) == n_waves) |>
  ungroup()

# Mixed model using all available data
m_all   <- lmer(Y_obs ~ wave * treatment + (wave | person), data = df_long)
m_complete <- lmer(Y_obs ~ wave * treatment + (wave | person), data = df_complete)

bind_rows(
  broom.mixed::tidy(m_all,      effects = "fixed") |> filter(str_detect(term, ":")) |>
    mutate(model = "MLM (all available data)"),
  broom.mixed::tidy(m_complete, effects = "fixed") |> filter(str_detect(term, ":")) |>
    mutate(model = "MLM (complete cases only)")
) |>
  select(model, estimate, std.error, p.value) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "Treatment × wave interaction: using all data reduces bias from selective dropout")

title: 42  Missing Data in Nested Contexts
text:
43.5 Multiple Imputation for Multilevel Data
When MCAR/MAR assumptions hold and you want to impute missing values before analysis (e.g., for predictors that are missing), the mice package supports multilevel-aware multiple imputation.
baseline_obs is a Level-2 variable — it is measured once per person, not once per observation. The right strategy is to impute it at the person level (one row per person), then merge the imputations back into the long-format data. This respects the nesting structure and avoids falsely treating the same missing value as if it were independent across waves.


Code
# Introduce 15% missingness in the person-level baseline covariate
set.seed(2024)
df_long_miss <- df_long |>
  group_by(person) |>
  mutate(
    baseline_obs = ifelse(runif(1) < 0.15, NA_real_, first(baseline))
  ) |>
  ungroup()

# --- Step 1: Impute at the person level ---
# Extract one row per person (Level-2 dataset)
df_person <- df_long_miss |>
  group_by(person) |>
  summarise(
    treatment    = first(treatment),
    baseline_obs = first(baseline_obs),
    mean_Y       = mean(Y_obs, na.rm = TRUE),  # person-level auxiliary variable
    .groups      = "drop"
  )

# Standard mice on the person-level data (pmm = predictive mean matching)
imp_L2 <- mice(
  df_person |> select(treatment, baseline_obs, mean_Y),
  method = "pmm",
  m      = 5,
  maxit  = 5,
  print  = FALSE
)

# --- Step 2: Merge each imputed L2 dataset back into the long-format data ---
# and run the mixed model, then pool
models_imp <- lapply(1:5, function(i) {
  df_person_imp <- df_person |>
    mutate(baseline_obs = complete(imp_L2, i)$baseline_obs)

  df_merged <- df_long_miss |>
    select(person, wave, treatment, Y_obs) |>
    left_join(df_person_imp |> select(person, baseline_obs),
              by = "person")

  lmer(Y_obs ~ wave * treatment + baseline_obs + (1 | person),
       data = df_merged, REML = FALSE)
})

pooled <- pool(models_imp)
summary(pooled) |>
  select(term, estimate, std.error, p.value) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "Pooled MI estimates: imputing at the person level respects nesting structure")

WarningAssumption: MAR (for Imputation and MLM)



What it means: The probability of a value being missing depends only on observed variables. This allows valid inference using all available data or multiple imputation.
How to check: You cannot directly test MAR vs. MNAR. Conduct sensitivity analyses: how different would your conclusions be under a range of MNAR scenarios? The tipping point analysis asks how extreme the missing data would have to be to overturn your result.
